title: Adaptive, Matrix-Free Low-Rank Approximation
summary: We study fixed-tolerance low-rank approximation in the matrix-free setting, where a matrix or linear operator $\mathbf{A}$ is accessible only through matrix-vector products and its rank must be determined adaptively to meet a prescribed error tolerance. We introduce a family of adaptive, matrix-free randomized QB algorithms. A randomized error indicator estimates the residual norm -- in either the Frobenius or the spectral norm -- directly from a random sketch, remaining accurate down to machine precision. A matrix-free rank-pruning step decouples the computational block size from the final rank, so that large, BLAS-3-friendly blocks can be used without over-estimating the rank, and an adjoint-free variant returns the orthonormal basis using only the forward operator. Across test matrices with diverse singular-value decays, the proposed methods attain ranks close to the truncated-SVD optimum while meeting the prescribed tolerance with high probability.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces adaptive, matrix-free randomized QB algorithms for fixed-tolerance low-rank approximation, using randomized error indicators and rank-pruning to achieve near-optimal ranks with high probability.
AI.motivation: To develop low-rank approximation methods that work in the matrix-free setting (only matrix-vector products) and adaptively determine rank to meet a prescribed error tolerance, without requiring the full matrix or SVD.
AI.method: A family of adaptive randomized QB algorithms with: (1) a randomized error indicator that estimates residual norm (Frobenius or spectral) directly from a random sketch, (2) a matrix-free rank-pruning step that decouples block size from final rank, and (3) an adjoint-free variant that returns orthonormal basis using only the forward operator.
AI.result: The proposed methods achieve ranks close to the truncated-SVD optimum while meeting prescribed error tolerance with high probability, across diverse singular-value decay patterns.
AI.conclusion: Effective matrix-free low-rank approximation is achievable with adaptive rank selection, accurate error estimation down to machine precision, and efficient BLAS-3-friendly computation.

title: On the order of Runge Kutta methods reusing last stage
summary:
In this paper we consider explicit Runge--Kutta (RK) methods for the numerical solution of Initial Value Problems (IVPs) in differential equations in which the last function evaluation in a step is reused, substituting the first evaluation for the next step. This requirement implies that, except for the first step, the computational cost of an $s$--stage explicit RK method reduces from $s$ to $(s-1)$ function evaluations per step. It will be seen that, in general, if a RK method has order $p$, when introducing this reused stage the new method has in general order $<p$.
  In this context we study the conditions on the coefficients of an explicit RK method with order $p$ such that the global error of the method reusing the last stage has the same order $p$ of accuracy. A detailed study on the conditions that the coefficients of an $s$ stage method with order $p$ must satisfy for the method reusing the last stage has also order $p$. In particular, for the families of $s$--stage explicit RK methods with the highest order $p=s$ we identify those methods reusing the last stage which retain the order of of the original method. The results of some numerical experiments are presented to confirm the theoretical results.

AI.tldr: This paper studies explicit Runge-Kutta methods where the last function evaluation in a step is reused as the first evaluation in the next step, reducing computational cost from s to (s-1) evaluations per step, and investigates conditions under which the original order of accuracy p is preserved.
AI.motivation: To reduce computational cost of explicit RK methods by reusing function evaluations between steps, while maintaining the original order of accuracy.
AI.method: The authors analyze the conditions on RK coefficients that allow reusing the last stage while preserving order p. They focus on s-stage methods with the highest possible order p=s and identify which such methods retain order after reuse.
AI.result: Generally, reusing the last stage reduces the order below p. Specific coefficient conditions are derived for preserving order p. For s-stage methods with p=s, certain methods retain the original order when reusing the last stage.
AI.conclusion: Theoretical conditions for order preservation are established and confirmed by numerical experiments.

title: A locking free mixed FEM based on a pure pseudostress based formulation for the elasticity eigenproblem
summary: We analyze a novel locking-free mixed formulation for the elasticity eigenvalue problem in both two and three dimensions, expressed exclusively in terms of the pseudostress tensor. An important feature of this formulation is that it does not require the enforcement of symmetry, either in a weak or strong sense. The displacement of the structure is recovered via a postprocess of the computed pseudostress. We introduce a mixed finite element method based in the tensorial version of the standard families of finite elements to discretize the space $\boldsymbol{\mathcal{H}}(\bdiv)$. We prove convergence and a priori error estimates under the theory of non-compact operators. Additionally, we perform an a posteriori error analysis for the problem, proving reliability and efficiency of the proposed indicator. We validate our theoretical results with numerical tests on different geometrical and physical configurations.

AI.tldr: A locking-free mixed finite element method for the elasticity eigenvalue problem using pseudostress tensor (without symmetry enforcement) is proposed, with convergence analysis under non-compact operator theory and reliable/efficient a posteriori error estimates, validated by numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: To develop a locking-free formulation for the elasticity eigenvalue problem that avoids the complexity of enforcing symmetry in the stress tensor, using a pseudostress-based mixed approach.
AI.method: Mixed finite element method using tensorial versions of standard $\mathcal{H}(\bdiv)$ finite element families, with displacement recovered via postprocessing; a priori error analysis via non-compact operator theory; a posteriori error analysis with reliability and efficiency proofs.
AI.result: Convergence and a priori error estimates are proved; a posteriori error indicator is shown to be both reliable and efficient; numerical tests confirm theoretical findings across various geometries and physical parameters.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pseudostress-based mixed formulation provides a viable locking-free approach for elasticity eigenvalue problems without symmetry constraints, supported by rigorous error analysis and numerical validation.

title: RPLSS: A randomized projected linear systems solver
summary: The projected linear system solver (PLSS), by incrementally appending columns to a random or deterministic sketching matrix, provides an attractive finite termination property for consistent linear systems. Nevertheless, a critical computational bottleneck of PLSS is accessing the whole coefficient matrix per iteration, making it prohibitive for extremely large-scale problems or applications with missing data. To alleviate this limitation, we propose a unified randomized PLSS (RPLSS) framework, built upon the tailored randomized row or column selection strategies that require only partial matrix information per iteration, for solving a general linear system, whether it is under- or overdetermined, and whether it is consistent or not. Within this framework, we develop a randomized Gaussian Kaczmarz method and its extended variant as row-action solvers, and randomized coordinate descent variants as column-action solvers. Theoretically, we prove that our methods inherit the finite termination property of PLSS, while achieving an exponential convergence rate, overcoming the sluggish convergence inherent in conventional randomized Kaczmarz and coordinate descent methods. Numerical experiments demonstrate the superiority of our method against state-of-the-art randomized methods, particularly in scenarios with large missing data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a unified Randomized Projected Linear System Solver (RPLSS) framework that uses partial matrix information per iteration to solve general linear systems (under/overdetermined, consistent/inconsistent). It develops randomized Gaussian Kaczmarz and coordinate descent variants that achieve finite termination and exponential convergence, outperforming existing methods especially with missing data.
AI.motivation: To overcome the computational bottleneck of PLSS requiring full matrix access per iteration, which is prohibitive for large-scale problems or applications with missing data.
AI.method: A unified RPLSS framework with randomized row/column selection strategies using only partial matrix information per iteration. Includes randomized Gaussian Kaczmarz methods (row-action) and randomized coordinate descent variants (column-action).
AI.result: The methods inherit PLSS's finite termination property and achieve exponential convergence rate, overcoming sluggish convergence of traditional randomized Kaczmarz and coordinate descent methods. Superior performance demonstrated numerically, especially with large missing data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RPLSS framework successfully addresses the scalability issue of PLSS while maintaining its theoretical guarantees, offering efficient solvers for large-scale linear systems with missing data.

title: Energy transfer and conversion in Strongly Anisotropic Magnetohydrodynamic Turbulence
summary: In homogeneous magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) turbulence without a background magnetic field driven by mechanical forces, an exact decomposition of the energy fluxes (D. Capocci et al., Journal of Plasma Physics, 91(1), E11 (2025)) has shown that current-sheet thinning is the dominant physical mechanism responsible for transferring energy from large to small scales. In contrast, mechanisms that are characteristic of hydrodynamic turbulence, such as vortex stretching and strain self-amplification, are strongly suppressed. Here, we extend this analysis to MHD turbulence in the presence of weak and strong imposed magnetic field, as previously driven by mechanical forces, and confirm that current-sheet thinning remains the leading process driving the energy cascade toward smaller scales in these more realistic configurations, and find enhanced scale invariance in the subfluxes. In addition to that, a decomposition of the contributions from the fluctuating and the background magnetic field to the conversion between kinetic and magnetic energies shows that the background-field-dependent contribution results in a nonlinear dynamo, that is an effective kinetic-to-magnetic conversion at large and intermediate scales. However, at small scales, it has the opposite effect, resulting in a net conversion of magnetic to kinetic energy.

AI.tldr: This paper extends prior analysis of energy transfer in MHD turbulence, confirming that current-sheet thinning (not vortex stretching) dominates the energy cascade even with imposed magnetic fields, while also revealing a scale-dependent nonlinear dynamo effect from the background field.
AI.motivation: To understand the dominant physical mechanisms driving the energy cascade in MHD turbulence under more realistic conditions (weak and strong imposed magnetic fields), extending previous findings from the homogeneous field-free case.
AI.method: Exact decomposition of energy fluxes in MHD turbulence simulations with imposed magnetic fields of varying strengths, analyzing subflux contributions and kinetic-magnetic energy conversion.
AI.result: Current-sheet thinning remains the leading energy cascade mechanism; vortex stretching and strain self-amplification are suppressed; enhanced scale invariance is found in subfluxes; a nonlinear dynamo converts kinetic to magnetic energy at large/intermediate scales but reverses at small scales.
AI.conclusion: Current-sheet thinning is the universal driver of energy transfer across scales in MHD turbulence, and the background magnetic field introduces a scale-dependent nonlinear dynamo effect that reverses direction at small scales.

title: Impact of Courant number on the results of numerical simulating of signal propagation in non-dispersive homogeneous media
comment: This is an English translation of the article published in Proceedings of the Komi Science Centre of the Ural Branch of the Russian Academy of Sciences. Series "Physical and Mathematical Sciences"
summary: The paper is devoted to the study of the connection between the numerical dispersion arising in FDTD modeling of electromagnetic signal propagation in nondispersive homogeneous media optically different from vacuum and the Courant number in the 2D case. The main results are formulated in the form of four statements, as well as a number of corollaries and remarks that determine the nature of the numerical dispersion, the optimal value of the Courant number and the limitations of the method. It is proved that the optimal choice of the Courant number eliminates the numerical dispersion and extends the capabilities of the developed numerical algorithm to media, which refractive index lesser than refractive index of vacuum, as well as media with negative refraction.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how numerical dispersion in 2D FDTD simulations of electromagnetic waves relates to the Courant number, proving that optimal Courant number selection eliminates numerical dispersion and extends the method to media with refractive indices less than vacuum or negative refraction.
AI.motivation: To understand the connection between numerical dispersion in FDTD modeling and the Courant number, and to find optimal conditions that eliminate numerical dispersion in nondispersive homogeneous media.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of numerical dispersion in 2D FDTD simulations, formulating results as four statements with corollaries and remarks.
AI.result: Optimal Courant number choice eliminates numerical dispersion and extends the algorithm to media with refractive indices less than vacuum and negative refraction.
AI.conclusion: Proper Courant number selection is key to eliminating numerical dispersion and broadening FDTD applicability to exotic media.

title: Infinity-harmonic functions and inverse mean curvature flow clusters
summary:
An $\infty$-harmonic function is a viscosity solution of $\nabla^2 u(\nabla u,\nabla u)=0$, or equivalently, an absolute minimizer of $\|\nabla u\|_{L^\infty}$. We prove a variety of new structural and regularity results in two dimensions, including:
  1. $\infty$-harmonic functions in domains of $\mathbb{R}^2$ are $C^{1,1/3}$.
  2. Critical points are isolated, and at each critical point, the solution has a unique quasiradial blow-up.
  3. Entire solutions with polynomial growth have unique quasiradial blow-downs, and are determined by their Fourier modes at infinity.
  These results are consequences of a new theory relating $\infty$-harmonic functions to inverse mean curvature flow (IMCF) clusters -- which are piecewise weak solutions of IMCF with common obstacle-type boundary conditions on the interfaces (a simple example is an embedded family of cuspidal curves evolving by inverse curvature). This connection arises as the $p\to\infty$ limit of the classical duality between $p$-harmonic and $q$-harmonic functions in $\mathbb{R}^2$, where $\frac1p+\frac1q=1$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that ∞-harmonic functions in ℝ² are C^{1,1/3}, critical points are isolated with unique quasiradial blow-ups, and entire solutions with polynomial growth have unique quasiradial blow-downs, all via a new connection to inverse mean curvature flow (IMCF) clusters.
AI.motivation: To establish new structural and regularity results for ∞-harmonic functions in two dimensions, which have been open problems, by developing a novel connection to inverse mean curvature flow.
AI.method: The authors develop a theory linking ∞-harmonic functions to IMCF clusters (piecewise weak solutions of IMCF with obstacle-type boundary conditions), arising as the p→∞ limit of the duality between p-harmonic and q-harmonic functions in ℝ² (1/p+1/q=1).
AI.result: (1) C^{1,1/3} regularity; (2) isolated critical points with unique quasiradial blow-up; (3) entire polynomial-growth solutions have unique quasiradial blow-downs and are determined by their Fourier modes at infinity.
AI.conclusion: The IMCF cluster framework provides a powerful new tool for analyzing ∞-harmonic functions, yielding sharp regularity and structural properties in two dimensions.

title: Solution Analysis of Tensor Equation $\mathcal{A} \ltimes \mathcal{X} \ltimes \mathcal{B}= \mathcal{C}$ via Semi Tensor Product with t-product
summary: This paper focuses on the analysis of the tensor equation $\mathcal{A\ltimes X\ltimes B=C}$, formulated via the semi tensor product with t-product. For the unknown vector $\mathcal{X}$, we establish a necessary and sufficient condition that provides an equivalence criterion for the existence of solutions. For matrix valued and higher-order tensor valued unknown $\mathcal{X}$, solvability is determined by corresponding compatibility requirements. Moreover, the explicit structure (Toeplitz and Circulant) of $\mathcal{C}$ is characterized. The derived results are supported by several illustrative examples.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the tensor equation A⋉X⋊B=C using semi-tensor product with t-product, providing necessary and sufficient conditions for solvability across different unknown types (vector, matrix, higher-order tensor) and characterizing the explicit structure of C.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous existence criteria and structural characterizations for tensor equations formulated via the semi-tensor product with t-product, which is a relatively new and less-explored tensor operation.
AI.method: The paper derives necessary and sufficient conditions for solution existence: equivalence criterion for vector X, and compatibility requirements for matrix/higher-order tensor X. It also characterizes the Toeplitz and Circulant structure of C.
AI.result: Clear conditions for solvability are established: an equivalence criterion for vector unknowns, compatibility conditions for matrix/tensor unknowns, and explicit structural characterization of C (Toeplitz, Circulant).
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a complete theoretical framework for solving tensor equations under semi-tensor product with t-product, supported by illustrative examples.

title: Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Hydrogen Plasma Smelting Reduction of iron ore: A Multi-Species Diagnostic Approach
summary: Plasma-based mineral-processing routes, such as hydrogen plasma smelting reduction (HPSR), which converts iron-ore fines directly to liquid metal in a single scalable step are commonly modeled by treating the arc as a spatially uniform heat source. Yet the reduction chemistry is governed by the strongly non-uniform conditions at the plasma-melt interface, which spatially averaged diagnostics cannot resolve. Here we spatially and temporally resolve the arc of a transferred arc HPSR reactor using multi-species optical emission spectroscopy (OES), in which neutral and ionic argon (Ar I, Ar II), hydrogen Balmer, and neutral iron (Fe I) emissions serve as intrinsic spatial filters set by their differing ionization thresholds. Combined with infrared thermography of the melt surface and an LTE thermal-plasma model validated against the benchmark free-burning argon arc, the measurements reveal a strongly stratified, non-isothermal discharge: an argon-defined core (>10,000 K), a partially recombined Balmer envelope (7,000-10,000 K), and an Fe I-traced interfacial boundary layer (3,000-4,000 K) directly above a melt surface at ~1,900-2,300 K. Across this steep thermal drop, positive hydrogen ions recombine before reaching the surface, so the reductant flux delivered to the oxide is overwhelmingly neutral; atomic hydrogen (H) and vibrationally excited molecular hydrogen H2(v), rather than the energetic ions often assumed. The measured electron density and excitation temperature bound the interfacial ionization. These findings redefine the boundary conditions for kinetic modeling of plasma-based ore reduction and establish a spatially resolved multi-species diagnostic framework transferable across plasma mineral-processing systems.

AI.tldr: This paper uses multi-species optical emission spectroscopy and infrared thermography to spatially and temporally resolve the arc in a hydrogen plasma smelting reduction reactor, revealing a strongly stratified discharge where hydrogen ions recombine before reaching the melt surface, delivering predominantly neutral atomic and vibrationally excited molecular hydrogen as the reductant rather than energetic ions.
AI.motivation: Existing models treat the plasma arc as a spatially uniform heat source, but the reduction chemistry is governed by strongly non-uniform conditions at the plasma-melt interface that spatial averaging cannot resolve, creating a need for high-resolution diagnostic techniques.
AI.method: Multi-species optical emission spectroscopy (OES) using neutral/ionic argon, hydrogen Balmer, and neutral iron emissions as intrinsic spatial filters, combined with infrared thermography of the melt surface and an LTE thermal-plasma model validated against a benchmark free-burning argon arc.
AI.result: The arc is strongly stratified: an argon-defined core (>10,000 K), a partially recombined Balmer envelope (7,000-10,000 K), and an Fe I-traced interfacial boundary layer (3,000-4,000 K) above a melt surface at ~1,900-2,300 K. Positive hydrogen ions recombine before reaching the surface, so the reductant flux is overwhelmingly neutral atomic hydrogen (H) and vibrationally excited H₂(v).
AI.conclusion: These findings redefine boundary conditions for kinetic modeling of plasma-based ore reduction and establish a spatially resolved multi-species diagnostic framework transferable across plasma mineral-processing systems.

title: Path Integral Monte Carlo for Fictitious Identical Particles with ξ-Ensemble
summary: In this work, a path integral Monte Carlo (PIMC) algorithm for fictitious identical particles (FIP) is proposed by introducing a $ξ$-ensemble and performing PIMC simulations on the resulting $ξ$-ensemble partition function. The PIMC algorithm with the $ξ$-ensemble allows us to obtain the thermodynamic properties of FIP for different $ξ$ values in a single simulation. Moreover, it also accelerates the simulation by improving the sampling efficiency, compared to the usual case where independent simulations are performed for each $ξ$ value, in the sense that the autocorrelation time of samples belonging to the same $ξ$ sector is decreased. Simulations of the uniform electron gas and uniform warm dense beryllium are performed to validate the improved algorithm and study the improvement in its sampling efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a path integral Monte Carlo (PIMC) algorithm for fictitious identical particles (FIP) using a ξ-ensemble, enabling single-simulation access to thermodynamic properties across different ξ values while improving sampling efficiency by reducing autocorrelation times. Validated on uniform electron gas and warm dense beryllium.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient PIMC algorithm for fictitious identical particles that can obtain thermodynamic properties for multiple ξ values in a single simulation and improve sampling efficiency compared to running independent simulations per ξ value.
AI.method: The method introduces a ξ-ensemble and derives a corresponding partition function, then performs PIMC simulations on this ξ-ensemble. This allows simultaneous sampling across multiple ξ values within one simulation run.
AI.result: The algorithm successfully obtains thermodynamic properties for different ξ values in a single simulation and reduces autocorrelation time for samples within the same ξ sector, validated on uniform electron gas and warm dense beryllium systems.
AI.conclusion: The ξ-ensemble PIMC algorithm is an effective approach for studying fictitious identical particles, offering both computational efficiency gains (single simulation for multiple ξ values) and improved sampling quality (reduced autocorrelation).

title: $H$-convergence and $Γ$-convergence in the Riesz fractional setting: the nonlinear case
summary: This paper concerns the $H$-convergence of nonlinear nonlocal monotone operators defined through the Riesz fractional gradient and divergence. We show that the $H$-convergence in this nonlocal framework is equivalent to the $H$-convergence of the corresponding local one. As a consequence, we obtain a $H$-compactness result for a suitable class of nonlocal monotone operators. We then study the $Γ$-convergence of nonlocal energy functionals associated with the subclass of \emph{conservative} monotone operators, proving that it is equivalent to the $Γ$-convergence of the corresponding local energies. A key ingredient is a new uniqueness result for the integral representation of both local and nonlocal functionals. As a by-product, we obtain the $Γ$-compactness of the class of nonlocal energies under consideration. Finally, we show the equivalence between the $H$-convergence of nonlocal conservative monotone operators and the $Γ$-convergence of the associated energy functionals.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes that H-convergence of nonlocal monotone operators (using Riesz fractional gradients) is equivalent to H-convergence of corresponding local operators, with analogous results for Γ-convergence of associated energy functionals.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between nonlocal (fractional) and local theories of monotone operators by proving equivalence results for H-convergence and Γ-convergence.
AI.method: Uses H-convergence and Γ-convergence frameworks, a new uniqueness result for integral representations of local/nonlocal functionals, and equivalence proofs connecting nonlocal and local settings.
AI.result: H-convergence of nonlocal monotone operators ⇔ H-convergence of local operators; Γ-convergence of nonlocal energies ⇔ Γ-convergence of local energies; H-compactness and Γ-compactness are obtained as consequences.
AI.conclusion: Nonlocal and local convergence theories are equivalent for this class of operators, unifying two previously separate analytical frameworks.

title: Numerical solutions of an accurate diffuse interface model of the incompressible resistive MHD free surface flow
summary: In this paper, we derive a new model to simulate the incompressible resistive magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) free surface flow. A thermodynamically consistent diffuse interface method is adopted to characterize the moving interface in the modeling process. The formal convergence of the proposed MHD free surface flow model to the sharp interface model is established via a matched asymptotic argument, and the model can be solved without the need for sophisticated free surface capturing schemes. We design a fully decoupled linear finite element scheme that preserves the divergence-free constraint of the magnetic field at a discrete level. The reliability and robustness of the proposed model and algorithm are validated through numerical investigations of the magnetic damping effect on bubble dynamics. In particular, we provide a quantitative numerical comparison of the present results with those obtained from an inductionless MHD model and a sharp interface arbitrary Lagrangian--Eulerian model.

AI.tldr: A new diffuse interface model for incompressible resistive MHD free surface flow is derived, with proven convergence to sharp interface models, a fully decoupled linear finite element scheme preserving divergence-free magnetic fields, and numerical validation on magnetic damping effects in bubble dynamics.
AI.motivation: To develop a thermodynamically consistent model for simulating incompressible resistive MHD free surface flows that avoids sophisticated free surface capturing schemes while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: A diffuse interface method based on thermodynamic consistency is used to characterize moving interfaces. Formal convergence to sharp interface models is shown via matched asymptotic analysis. A fully decoupled linear finite element scheme is designed to preserve the divergence-free constraint of the magnetic field.
AI.result: Numerical validation demonstrates the model's reliability and robustness in capturing magnetic damping effects on bubble dynamics. Quantitative comparisons against inductionless MHD and sharp interface ALE models are provided.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model and algorithm offer a practical, accurate approach to simulating MHD free surface flows without complex free surface tracking, with good agreement to established models.

title: A Variational Framework for Guiding-Center Kinetics, Anisotropic Equilibria, and Quasisymmetry in Stellarators
summary:
We present a variational framework in which (i) guiding-center kinetic theory, (ii) macroscopic force balance with gyrotropic/anisotropic pressure, and (iii) quasisymmetry (QS) constraints appear as different facets of a single structure. Starting from a guiding-center Vlasov--Maxwell action, constrained variations yield the guiding-center kinetic equation and Maxwell equations. Without phenomenological closure, momentum conservation yields macroscopic force balance $\mathbf{J} 	imes \mathbf{B}/c = 
abla\cdot oldsymbolΠ$, where $\mathbf{J}$ is the current density, $\mathbf{B}$ is the magnetic field, and $ oldsymbolΠ$ is the gyrotropic stress tensor. We connect QS to an integrability condition expressed in coordinate-free form via $$f_T \equiv 
ablaψ\cdot  igl(
abla B 	imes 
abla(\mathbf{B} \cdot 
abla B) igr) = 0,$$ where $ψ$ is the flux surface label, and show how this condition leads to solvability constraints on anisotropy closely related to those found in a recent constrained Kruskal--Kulsrud variational formulation.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a variational framework unifying guiding-center kinetic theory, macroscopic force balance with gyrotropic pressure, and quasisymmetry (QS) constraints. It derives the guiding-center kinetic equation and Maxwell equations from a constrained Vlasov-Maxwell action, shows momentum conservation yields force balance without phenomenological closure, and connects QS to a coordinate-free integrability condition that imposes constraints on anisotropy.
AI.motivation: To develop a unified variational framework that connects guiding-center kinetic theory, macroscopic force balance with anisotropic pressure, and quasisymmetry constraints, enabling a first-principles derivation of closure relations and solvability conditions for anisotropy in stellarator design.
AI.method: Starting from a guiding-center Vlasov-Maxwell action, constrained variations are performed to derive the guiding-center kinetic equation and Maxwell equations. Momentum conservation is then used to obtain macroscopic force balance with a gyrotropic stress tensor without phenomenological closure. Quasisymmetry is linked to an integrability condition expressed in coordinate-free form via f_T = ∇ψ·(∇B × ∇(B·∇B)) = 0.
AI.result: The framework yields: (1) guiding-center kinetic equation and Maxwell equations from variational principles, (2) macroscopic force balance J×B/c = ∇·Π with gyrotropic stress tensor without closure assumptions, and (3) a coordinate-free quasisymmetry condition f_T = 0 that leads to solvability constraints on anisotropy.
AI.conclusion: The variational approach unifies kinetic and fluid descriptions of magnetized plasmas with anisotropy, and the coordinate-free QS condition provides solvability constraints linking quasisymmetry to pressure anisotropy, relating to the constrained Kruskal-Kulsrud formulation.

title: Radiation Damage Cascades in Fullerite Using Molecular Dynamics
summary: Molecular dynamics is used to study radiation cascades in solid C60 under ambient conditions. Simulations are performed for Primary Knock-On Atom (PKA) energies from 0.1 to 1 keV, and cascades are sampled over many PKA directions to collect statistics. Energies and forces are described using the Environment Dependent Interaction Potential for carbon paired with the Ziegler-Biersack-Littmark potential for short-range interactions, and cascade behaviour is characterized by tracking kinetic energy, hybridization and bond connectivity as a function of time. Compared to most materials, fullerite exhibits an unusual radiation response due to weak thermal transfer between C60 molecules leading to a thermalization phase lasting hundreds of picoseconds. The cascades damage the C60 molecules and link them together, and a linear relation is found between the number of cross-linked molecules and the number of new sp3 atoms. The threshold displacement energy computed is 18 eV, in agreement with experiments

AI.tldr: This study uses molecular dynamics to simulate radiation cascades in solid C60 (fullerite), finding that it exhibits unusual radiation response due to weak thermal transfer between molecules, with a linear relationship between cross-linked molecules and new sp3 atoms, and a threshold displacement energy of 18 eV.
AI.motivation: To understand the radiation response of solid C60 (fullerite), which exhibits unusual behavior compared to most materials due to weak thermal transfer between C60 molecules.
AI.method: Molecular dynamics simulations for PKA energies from 0.1 to 1 keV with sampling over many PKA directions, using Environment Dependent Interaction Potential for carbon paired with Ziegler-Biersack-Littmark potential for short-range interactions.
AI.result: The cascades damage C60 molecules and link them together, with a linear relation between cross-linked molecules and new sp3 atoms. The thermalization phase lasts hundreds of picoseconds. Threshold displacement energy is 18 eV, matching experiments.
AI.conclusion: Fullerite shows a unique radiation response characterized by long thermalization times and cross-linking behavior, with a computed threshold displacement energy consistent with experimental measurements.

title: Power Convexity of Solutions to the Complex Monge-Ampère Equation $\det(u_{i\overline{j}})=1$ in Complex Dimension Two
summary: In this paper, we establish the power convexity of solutions to the complex Monge-Ampère equation $\det(u_{i\overline{j}})=1$ in a convex set in the complex 2 dimensional Euclidean space. Even the convexity result is only for 2 dimension, our approach is applicable to all dimensions.

AI.tldr: The paper proves power convexity of solutions to the complex Monge-Ampère equation in complex 2D convex sets, with a method extendable to higher dimensions.
AI.motivation: To establish power convexity properties for solutions of the complex Monge-Ampère equation, which is a fundamental PDE in complex geometry and analysis.
AI.method: The proof is carried out in complex 2-dimensional space, but the approach is designed to be dimension-independent and applicable in all dimensions.
AI.result: Power convexity of solutions is established for the complex Monge-Ampère equation in convex domains in C².
AI.conclusion: The power convexity result holds in dimension 2, and the method generalizes to arbitrary dimensions.

title: Mixed precision explicit numerical methods for ordinary differential equations
summary: Our objective is to solve large systems of ordinary differential equations (ODEs) commonly used to model biological processes. These equations are typically nonlinear, complex, and high-dimensional. In computational biology, such ODEs are generally solved using numerical methods. In this work, we focus on explicit numerical methods because of their flexibility. However, their limited stability regions may result in high computational costs. To mitigate this issue, we investigate mixed precision algorithms designed to reduce computational effort by performing selected parts of the numerical method in lower arithmetic precision. We develop several mixed precision explicit methods and assess their performance on two large scale biological benchmark ODE models. Our theoretical analysis highlights the effectiveness of partially reducing arithmetic precision within explicit methods. Numerical experiments demonstrate that our mixed methods-implemented in both sequential and parallel versions using MPI-combining single (float) and double precision arithmetic can achieve up to twice the speed of a fully double precision implementation while preserving the same level of accuracy. Furthermore, the results indicate that decreasing the timestep improves the performance and robustness of our mixed methods, while the single precision method fails to converge.

AI.tldr: This paper develops mixed precision explicit numerical methods for solving large-scale ODE systems in computational biology, achieving up to 2x speedup over double precision by selectively using single precision arithmetic, while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Large systems of nonlinear ODEs used in biological modeling are computationally expensive to solve with explicit numerical methods due to stability constraints requiring small timesteps, motivating the need for computational speedups without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: The authors develop several mixed precision variants of explicit numerical methods, implementing them in both sequential and parallel (MPI) versions that combine single (float) and double precision arithmetic for different parts of the computation.
AI.result: Mixed precision methods achieve up to twice the speed of fully double precision implementations while preserving the same accuracy level on two large-scale biological benchmark ODE models. Decreasing the timestep improves performance and robustness, while pure single precision fails to converge.
AI.conclusion: Partially reducing arithmetic precision within explicit numerical methods is an effective strategy to reduce computational costs for biological ODE systems, with mixed precision offering a practical trade-off between speed and accuracy.

title: Ultrafast proton radiography of the magnetic fields generated by a laser-driven coil current
summary: Magnetic fields generated by a current flowing through a U-shaped coil connecting two copper foils were measured using ultrafast proton radiography. Two $\sim$1.25 kJ, 1-ns laser pulses propagated through laser entrance holes in the front foil, and were focused to the back foil with an intensity of $\sim$3 $\times$ 10$^{16}$ W$/$cm$^{2}$. The intense laser-solid interaction induced a high voltage between the copper foils and generated a large current in the connecting coil. The proton data show $\sim$40-50 Tesla magnetic fields at the center of the coil $\sim$3-4 ns after laser irradiation. The experiments provide significant insight for future target designs that aim to develop a powerful source of external magnetic fields for various applications in high-energy-density science.

AI.tldr: Researchers used ultrafast proton radiography to measure ~40-50 Tesla magnetic fields generated by a U-shaped coil connecting two copper foils, created by intense laser pulses (~3×10^16 W/cm²) inducing a high voltage and large current.
AI.motivation: To develop and characterize a powerful source of external magnetic fields for high-energy-density science applications.
AI.method: Two ~1.25 kJ, 1-ns laser pulses were focused on a back copper foil through laser entrance holes in a front foil, generating a high voltage between the foils and current in a U-shaped connecting coil. Magnetic fields were measured using ultrafast proton radiography.
AI.result: ~40-50 Tesla magnetic fields were measured at the center of the coil approximately 3-4 ns after laser irradiation.
AI.conclusion: The experiments provide significant insight for future target designs to develop powerful external magnetic field sources for high-energy-density science.

title: Inverse Low-Dimensional Manifold Reconstruction Framework for Spatiotemporal Reconstruction of Compressible Physical Fields
summary: Compressible physical fields are widely present in the real physical world, but current artificial intelligence lacks an understanding mechanism for the non-differentiable features in compressible physical fields. Addressing the limitations of existing deep learning architectures in handling global non-differentiable features, we propose the Inverse Low-Dimensional Manifold reconstruction framework (ILDM). This framework couples the Non-differentiable Approximation Function (NAF) for capturing non-differentiable features in compressible flows with the Smooth Fluid Reconstruction (SFR) module tailored for smooth fluid regions. Extensive evaluations across 1D and 2D benchmarks, including Riemann problems and double Mach reflection, demonstrate that ILDM significantly outperforms cPINN and R-adaptive DeepONet. Specifically, ILDM achieves superior localization of non-differentiable interfaces and maintains robust super-resolution performance even with low-resolution inputs, establishing a physically consistent and scalable paradigm for data-driven fluid dynamics.

AI.tldr: ILDM is a new deep learning framework that couples a Non-differentiable Approximation Function (NAF) and Smooth Fluid Reconstruction (SFR) module to handle non-differentiable features (like shock waves) in compressible physical fields, outperforming existing methods on Riemann problems and double Mach reflection benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current AI lacks understanding of non-differentiable features in compressible physical fields (e.g., shock waves, discontinuities), and existing deep learning architectures struggle to handle these global non-differentiable features effectively.
AI.method: Proposes Inverse Low-Dimensional Manifold reconstruction (ILDM) framework, which couples a Non-differentiable Approximation Function (NAF) for capturing discontinuities and a Smooth Fluid Reconstruction (SFR) module for smooth flow regions.
AI.result: ILDM significantly outperforms cPINN and R-adaptive DeepONet on 1D/2D benchmarks (Riemann problems, double Mach reflection), achieving superior localization of non-differentiable interfaces and robust super-resolution from low-resolution inputs.
AI.conclusion: ILDM establishes a physically consistent and scalable paradigm for data-driven fluid dynamics, effectively handling compressible flow discontinuities.

title: Nonlinear stability of a background magnetic field for the 3D compressible MHD equations with anisotropic dissipation
summary: We study the nonlinear stability of an equilibrium with a background magnetic field for the three-dimensional compressible magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) equations in the whole space $\mathbb{R}^3$, in the strongly anisotropic regime where the velocity is dissipated only in the horizontal directions and the magnetic field is diffused in a single direction. We prove that for initial data sufficiently close to the equilibrium in a Sobolev space, the system admits a unique global-in-time solution that remains close to the equilibrium and enjoys quantitative dissipation estimates. The proof overcomes the severe lack of dissipation through two mechanisms: the background magnetic field is shown to generate enhanced dissipation for the magnetic field and the density, while a nonlinear cancellation mechanism is devised to resolve the loss of vertical derivatives caused by the compressible coupling.

AI.tldr: The paper proves global stability and enhanced dissipation for 3D compressible MHD equations with anisotropic dissipation near a background magnetic field, using a nonlinear cancellation mechanism to handle lost vertical derivatives.
AI.motivation: To understand the nonlinear stability of MHD equilibria under strongly anisotropic dissipation, where standard dissipative mechanisms are absent in certain directions, which is relevant to magnetized plasmas in astrophysics and fusion.
AI.method: The analysis combines (1) enhanced dissipation from the background magnetic field acting on the magnetic field and density, and (2) a novel nonlinear cancellation mechanism that compensates for the loss of vertical derivative estimates caused by compressible coupling.
AI.result: For initial data sufficiently close to equilibrium in a Sobolev space, a unique global-in-time solution exists, remains near the equilibrium, and satisfies quantitative dissipation estimates.
AI.conclusion: Despite severe lack of dissipation (horizontal-only velocity dissipation, single-direction magnetic diffusion), global stability is achievable via background field effects and a cancellation mechanism, advancing the mathematical theory of anisotropic MHD systems.

title: Optimized Stencil Strategy for the Generalized Finite Difference Method: Application to Steady-State Non-Linear Problems
summary: We propose an optimized stencil strategy for the Generalized Finite Difference Method (GFDM) applied to non-linear problems. We take advantage of the flexibility of GFDM to engineer specific stencils by agglomerating nodes and balancing size with numerical accuracy. Previous work, focusing on the stencil construction for the linear convection-diffusion problem, showed that optimizing stencils and scaling parameters improves the scheme. The present study aims to investigate similar benefits for non-linear problems such as the Burgers' equations and the weakly compressible Navier-Stokes system.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an optimized stencil strategy for the Generalized Finite Difference Method (GFDM) applied to non-linear problems (Burgers' equations and weakly compressible Navier-Stokes), extending prior work on linear convection-diffusion to investigate benefits of stencil optimization for non-linear cases.
AI.motivation: To extend previous successful stencil optimization work from linear convection-diffusion problems to more challenging non-linear problems (Burgers' equations and weakly compressible Navier-Stokes).
AI.method: Optimized stencil strategy for GFDM that agglomerates nodes and balances stencil size with numerical accuracy, tailored for non-linear problems.
AI.result: Extends the benefits of stencil optimization from linear to non-linear problems, demonstrating improved scheme performance.
AI.conclusion: The optimized stencil strategy for GFDM yields similar benefits for non-linear problems as previously shown for linear convection-diffusion problems.

title: Magnetic field generation by the Rayleigh-Taylor instability in laser-driven planar plastic targets
summary: Magnetic fields generated by the Rayleigh-Taylor instability were measured in laser-accelerated planar foils using ultrafast proton radiography. Thin plastic foils were irradiated with $\sim$4-kJ, 2.5-ns laser pulses focused to an intensity of $\sim$10$^{14}$ W$/$cm$^{2}$ on the OMEGA EP Laser System. Target modulations were seeded by laser nonuniformities and amplified during target acceleration by the Rayleigh-Taylor instability. The experimental data show the hydrodynamic evolution of the target and MG-level magnetic fields generated in the broken foil. The experimental data are in good agreement with predictions from 2-D magnetohydrodynamic simulations.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the first direct measurements of magnetic fields generated by Rayleigh-Taylor instability in laser-accelerated foils using proton radiography, with MG-level fields observed, matching 2-D MHD simulations.
AI.motivation: To experimentally measure and characterize magnetic fields generated by the Rayleigh-Taylor instability in laser-driven plasmas, which is important for understanding instability growth and energy transport in inertial confinement fusion.
AI.method: Ultrafast proton radiography was used on OMEGA EP Laser System to irradiate thin plastic foils with ~4-kJ, 2.5-ns laser pulses at ~10^14 W/cm^2. Target modulations were seeded by laser nonuniformities and amplified by RT instability.
AI.result: MG-level magnetic fields were observed in the broken foil, and the hydrodynamic evolution was tracked. Experimental data showed good agreement with 2-D MHD simulations.
AI.conclusion: The Rayleigh-Taylor instability in laser-accelerated foils generates measurable MG-level magnetic fields, validating MHD simulation predictions for such high-energy-density systems.

title: Collaborate to decorrelate in path space: Hamiltonian replica exchange transition interface sampling (HRETIS)
summary: We present Hamiltonian Replica Exchange Transition Interface Sampling (HRETIS), a path sampling framework designed to efficiently sample rare events in systems with complex potential energy landscapes. HRETIS introduces a helper potential within a Hamiltonian replica exchange scheme, which enhances exploration of path space when the underlying potential is not well suited for conventional path sampling approaches. This is particularly advantageous for systems exhibiting multiple pathways separated by orthogonal barriers such as in drug (un)binding, where standard algorithms often show slow convergence since they become trapped within specific pathways. By exchanging Hamiltonians between the path ensembles, HRETIS overcomes these limitations and increases the decorrelation between subsequent paths in the Monte Carlo chain. We demonstrate that HRETIS provides robust and accurate kinetics in several systems, including coarse-grained simulations of amino acid permeation through a dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC) membrane. Moreover, HRETIS is found to improve sampling efficiency and convergence, illustrating its potential as a powerful tool for rare event sampling in complex molecular systems.

AI.tldr: HRETIS is a new path sampling framework combining Hamiltonian replica exchange with transition interface sampling to efficiently sample rare events in complex molecular systems, demonstrated on amino acid permeation through a DPPC membrane.
AI.motivation: Conventional path sampling methods converge slowly for systems with multiple pathways separated by orthogonal barriers (e.g., drug binding/unbinding) because they get trapped within specific pathways.
AI.method: HRETIS introduces a helper potential within a Hamiltonian replica exchange scheme, exchanging Hamiltonians between path ensembles to enhance exploration and decorrelation in the Monte Carlo chain.
AI.result: HRETIS provides robust and accurate kinetics in coarse-grained simulations of amino acid permeation through a DPPC membrane, and improves sampling efficiency and convergence.
AI.conclusion: HRETIS is a powerful tool for rare event sampling in complex molecular systems, overcoming limitations of standard path sampling algorithms.

title: Robust Observability for Schrödinger Equations with Rough Potentials on 2D Compact Hyperbolic Surfaces
summary: This paper investigates the robustness of quantum observability on compact hyperbolic surfaces under $L^2$ potential perturbations. Since $L^2$ regularity is strictly subcritical in dimension two, for every non-empty open set $Ω\subset M$ and $T>0$, the solution of $(i\partial_t+Δ-V)u=0$ satisfies the space-time observability estimate $$ \|u_0\|_{L^2(M)}^2 \leq C\int_0^T \|e^{-it(-Δ+V)}u_0\|_{L^2(Ω)}^2\,dt. $$ Our results provide a quantitative confirmation that the delocalization of high-energy quantum states on negatively curved manifolds is robust against $L^2$-class microscopic scattering. The proof combines the hyperbolic dynamics of the geodesic flow with semiclassical analysis. A key ingredient is an $L^4$ spectral cluster estimate with an arbitrarily small loss, obtained by exploiting Jacobi field analysis and Bourgain--Demeter $l^2$-decoupling. This estimate allows us to obtain refined spectral localized semiclassical Strichartz estimates adapted to rough potentials and to show that the potential's contribution vanishes in the propagation of semiclassical measures. The full-support property of invariant semiclassical measures on hyperbolic surfaces then yields the desired observability by contradiction. By the Hilbert Uniqueness Method, the corresponding internal controllability result follows.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that quantum observability on compact hyperbolic surfaces is robust under rough ($L^2$) potential perturbations, using semiclassical analysis and hyperbolic dynamics.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether high-energy quantum state delocalization on negatively curved manifolds remains stable when microscopic scattering potentials are introduced, specifically at the subcritical $L^2$ regularity level.
AI.method: Combines hyperbolic geodesic flow dynamics with semiclassical analysis; uses an $L^4$ spectral cluster estimate (via Jacobi fields and Bourgain-Demeter $l^2$-decoupling) to derive refined Strichartz estimates that handle rough potentials, then shows potential contributions vanish in semiclassical measure propagation.
AI.result: Established the space-time observability estimate for the Schrödinger equation with $L^2$ potential perturbations on compact hyperbolic surfaces, confirming robustness of quantum delocalization.
AI.conclusion: The delocalization of high-energy quantum states on negatively curved manifolds is robust against $L^2$-class microscopic scattering, and the corresponding internal controllability result follows via the Hilbert Uniqueness Method.

title: A framework for generating nonconforming triangular meshes with multiple discretization layers
summary: We present a framework for generating nonconforming triangular meshes with multiple discretization layers. The framework exploits characteristic structural properties of meshes produced by a frontal Delaunay algorithm with uniform element size. The bulk region of such meshes exhibits a structured pattern resembling a regular triangular lattice. Owing to this structure, the bulk region can be coarsened by grouping elements into connected subsets of larger composite elements. When applied repeatedly, this procedure produces a mesh with multiple discretization layers. For complex geometries, the framework can be used to create composite multidomain meshes, where multiple discretization layers are generated within each subdomain. We present examples of meshes generated by the proposed framework and discuss postprocessing strategies for their refinement and coarsening. The resulting meshes are well suited for the application of adaptive mesh refinement techniques. The proposed framework can be readily integrated with existing mesh generators and finite-element solvers that support nonconforming triangular meshes.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a framework for generating nonconforming triangular meshes with multiple discretization layers by exploiting the structured pattern of frontal Delaunay meshes, enabling grouping and coarsening of elements into composite elements for multidomain applications.
AI.motivation: To enable efficient generation of nonconforming triangular meshes with multiple discretization layers that can be used for adaptive mesh refinement and complex multidomain geometries.
AI.method: The framework exploits the structured pattern (regular triangular lattice) of bulk regions in frontal Delaunay meshes, coarsening them by grouping elements into larger composite elements repeatedly to form multiple discretization layers.
AI.result: The framework produces meshes suitable for adaptive mesh refinement and can be integrated with existing mesh generators and finite-element solvers supporting nonconforming triangular meshes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively generates multidomain meshes with multiple layers and is compatible with existing tools, supporting both refinement and coarsening postprocessing strategies.

title: Terahertz Generation through Photon Deceleration of Long-Wavelength Infrared Laser Pulses in Plasma
summary: Efficient terahertz (THz) generation with high field amplitude and pulse energy is studied through the interaction of a single-color long-wavelength infrared (LWIR) laser pulse with gaseous targets. Particle-In-Cell (PIC) simulations are performed to investigate the underlying mechanism and analyze the properties of the emitted THz radiation. The results reveal that THz pulses are generated via photon deceleration of the LWIR laser, driven by enhanced electron density accumulation at the pulse front in the self-modulated wakefield regime. The influence of key parameters, including target density, laser intensity, and propagation length, on nonlinear laser modulation and the resulting THz generation efficiency is analyzed. Important scalings governing the laser-to-THz energy conversion efficiency are identified from PIC simulations and validated through theoretical analysis. The study demonstrates a laser-to-THz energy conversion efficiency of approximately $4\%$, significantly exceeding previously reported values. The field amplitude of the emitted THz pulses is found to be on the order of 100 GV/m, with a pulse energy of approximately 50 mJ for the laser parameters considered in this work. The findings of this study provide valuable insights for the development of next-generation high-energy THz sources.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates efficient high-energy THz generation (~4% conversion efficiency, ~100 GV/m field, ~50 mJ energy) using single-color LWIR laser interaction with gas targets via photon deceleration in the self-modulated wakefield regime, validated by PIC simulations and theoretical analysis.
AI.motivation: To develop next-generation high-energy THz sources with significantly improved conversion efficiency and field amplitude, overcoming limitations of previously reported THz generation methods.
AI.method: PIC simulations of single-color LWIR laser interaction with gaseous targets; analysis of photon deceleration mechanism in self-modulated wakefield regime; systematic study of target density, laser intensity, and propagation length parameters.
AI.result: ~4% laser-to-THz energy conversion efficiency; THz field amplitude ~100 GV/m; THz pulse energy ~50 mJ; identification of key scalings for conversion efficiency validated by theory.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LWIR-driven THz generation scheme yields significantly higher efficiency and energy than previous reports, providing a promising path for next-generation high-energy THz sources.

title: Semilocal exchange functionals from the exact-exchange condition for the hydrogen atom: Hydrogenic exactness and recovery of Rydberg-like bound states
summary: In semilocal density functionals the exchange potential decays too rapidly outside atoms and molecules and lacks the correct $-1/r$ tail; as a consequence, functionals from PBE to modern meta-GGAs such as SCAN support no bound Rydberg-like state of an atom. We revisit the gradient-corrected exchange functional that Gill and Pople (GP93) constructed to reproduce the exact exchange potential of the hydrogen atom, solve their equation as an inverse problem, and use the resulting enhancement factor -- the GP93 factor -- as a tail-generating ingredient of a switched semilocal functional. The GP93 factor is exact on the hydrogen $1s$ density and, by uniform coordinate scaling, on hydrogenic $1s$ ions, where it yields the eigenvalue $-Z^2/2$ exactly (hydrogenic exactness). Its large-gradient growth has the hydrogen-exact form $O[s(\ln s)^{2/3}]$, distinct from the $s\ln s$ form of Armiento and Kümmel. The factor is combined with a kinetic-energy-density indicator so that its divergent branch acts only in one-electron-like regions; the GP93 ingredient is nonempirical, while a few switching parameters are calibrated to balance tail recovery against self-consistent-field stability. The resulting functional produces Rydberg-like series of bound virtual Kohn--Sham states in systems whose outermost shell is a one-electron-like $s$ shell -- all-electron H and He, and Li, Na, and K with large-core pseudopotentials -- and moves the highest-occupied eigenvalue toward the experimental ionization energy. For $p$-shell atoms (Ne, Ar) the switch closes, so the domain of applicability is fixed by design. Fixed-density tests indicate that a reduced-Laplacian switch distinguishes atomic tails from covalent bond centers, and that a density-only kinetic-energy functional renders the entire switch orbital-independent, so that no generalized Kohn--Sham solver is required.

AI.tldr: A semilocal density functional is developed using the GP93 exchange enhancement factor (exact for hydrogen 1s) to fix the missing -1/r tail in standard functionals (PBE, SCAN), enabling bound Rydberg-like virtual states and improving highest-occupied eigenvalues for one-electron-like s-shell systems, while remaining closed for p-shell atoms.
AI.motivation: Semilocal density functionals (PBE to SCAN) lack the correct -1/r exchange potential tail, thus cannot support bound Rydberg-like states of atoms. The authors aim to fix this by incorporating the GP93 exchange factor designed to reproduce the exact hydrogen exchange potential.
AI.method: The GP93 enhancement factor (exact for hydrogen 1s, scaling to hydrogenic ions) is revisited as an inverse problem. It is combined with a kinetic-energy-density indicator so its divergent branch activates only in one-electron-like regions. A few switching parameters are calibrated nonempirically to balance tail recovery and SCF stability.
AI.result: The functional produces bound Rydberg-like virtual KS states for systems with one-electron-like s-shells (H, He, Li, Na, K) and shifts HOMO eigenvalues toward experimental ionization energies. For p-shell atoms (Ne, Ar) the switch closes as intended. Fixed-density tests show a reduced-Laplacian switch distinguishes atomic tails from covalent bonds, and a density-only kinetic functional makes the switch orbital-independent.
AI.conclusion: The GP93-tailored semilocal functional successfully recovers the correct -1/r tail behavior for one-electron-like regions, enabling bound Rydberg states and improved eigenvalues, while the domain of applicability is fixed by design (active for s-shell, closed for p-shell atoms).

title: Gradient continuity for $p$-Laplacian obstacle problems under mean oscillation conditions
summary: We establish the $C^1$-regularity of solutions to the obstacle problems associated with $p$-Laplacian type equations, where $1<p<\infty$. Specifically, we prove that the gradient of the solution is continuous under a Dini mean oscillation ($\mathsf{DMO}$) type condition on the data, which includes the coefficient matrix, the source term, and the obstacle function. This result relaxes the classical Dini continuity assumption on the data to a more general mean oscillation condition.

AI.tldr: The paper proves that solutions to obstacle problems for p-Laplacian equations have continuous gradients (C^1-regularity) under a general Dini mean oscillation condition on the data, relaxing the classical Dini continuity requirement.
AI.motivation: To establish C^1-regularity for obstacle problems with p-Laplacian type equations under weaker assumptions than classical Dini continuity, specifically using a DMO-type condition on the coefficient matrix, source term, and obstacle function.
AI.method: The authors analyze obstacle problems for p-Laplacian type equations (1<p<∞) and prove gradient continuity under a Dini mean oscillation (DMO) condition on the data, generalizing the classical pointwise Dini continuity assumption.
AI.result: The gradient of the solution is shown to be continuous under the DMO condition on the coefficient matrix, source term, and obstacle function, which relaxes the classical Dini continuity assumption.
AI.conclusion: The C^1-regularity result holds under the more general mean oscillation condition, extending previous regularity theories for p-Laplacian obstacle problems.

title: Iterative finite element approximation of non-monotone semilinear diffusion-reaction equations
summary: We study iterative linearized finite element methods for the numerical approximation of semilinear elliptic boundary value problems with nonlinear reaction terms of asymptotically linear growth. Our approach reaches considerably beyond the classical theory by allowing for nonlinearities that are not necessarily monotone. We investigate a stabilized implicit-explicit (IMEX) iteration combined with first-order finite element discretizations on graded meshes that are able to resolve corner singularities in polygonal domains. To account for the reduced regularity of the analytical solutions, we establish regularity estimates in corner-weighted Sobolev spaces. The principal novelty of the paper is a non-standard analysis of the approximation properties of the discrete Galerkin limits resulting from the iterative process, which yields optimal a priori convergence estimates. Numerical experiments underline the theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: This paper develops and analyzes a stabilized IMEX finite element method for solving semilinear elliptic BVPs with asymptotically linear, non-monotone nonlinearities on polygonal domains, using graded meshes to handle corner singularities and proving optimal convergence via non-standard Galerkin analysis.
AI.motivation: Extend classical theory beyond monotone nonlinearities to handle broader classes of semilinear elliptic problems with asymptotically linear growth, while addressing corner singularities in polygonal domains.
AI.method: Stabilized implicit-explicit (IMEX) iteration combined with first-order finite element discretizations on graded meshes, analyzed via regularity estimates in corner-weighted Sobolev spaces and non-standard discrete Galerkin limit analysis.
AI.result: Achieves optimal a priori convergence estimates for the iterative linearized FEM, validated by numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully handles non-monotone nonlinearities and corner singularities, with rigorous optimal convergence guarantees.

title: Counter-streaming heat-flux closure for electron-only collisionless magnetic reconnection
summary: In electron-only collisionless magnetic reconnection (MR), a regime of growing importance in turbulent space plasmas, electrons develop strongly non-Maxwellian distributions that invalidate conventional fluid closures based on assumptions of near local thermodynamic equilibrium. Using particle-in-cell (PIC) simulations, we identify the physical origin of the electron heat-flux: counter-streaming between electron sub-populations originating from opposite sides of the current sheet, with each sub-population remaining approximately adiabatic. This insight yields a novel fluid closure, which we implement in fluid simulations using two adiabatic electron fluids initialized on opposite sides of the current sheet. The fluid simulations capture the heat-flux, reconnecting current density, thermal pressure, and bulk flows as observed in PIC, within a reduced fluid description that conventional single-electron-fluid models fundamentally cannot reproduce. The closure is most accurate at low $β_{\text{Reconn.}}$ and $B_{\text{Guide}}/B_{\text{Reconn.}}$, regimes relevant to Earth's magnetotail, where it establishes counter-streaming as the physical origin of heat-flux in electron-only collisionless MR and enables its computationally efficient fluid modeling.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies counter-streaming electron populations as the physical origin of heat-flux in electron-only collisionless magnetic reconnection, and develops a novel two-fluid adiabatic closure that accurately reproduces PIC simulation results, particularly in low-β and low-guide-field regimes relevant to Earth's magnetotail.
AI.motivation: Electron-only collisionless magnetic reconnection is increasingly important in turbulent space plasmas, but electrons develop strongly non-Maxwellian distributions that invalidate conventional single-fluid closures based on near-local thermodynamic equilibrium assumptions.
AI.method: Using PIC simulations to identify counter-streaming between electron sub-populations from opposite sides of the current sheet as the origin of heat-flux, then implementing a novel fluid closure with two adiabatic electron fluids initialized on opposite sides of the current sheet.
AI.result: The two-fluid model captures heat-flux, reconnecting current density, thermal pressure, and bulk flows as observed in PIC simulations, which conventional single-electron-fluid models fundamentally cannot reproduce.
AI.conclusion: Counter-streaming is established as the physical origin of heat-flux in electron-only collisionless MR, and the closure enables computationally efficient fluid modeling, most accurate at low β_Reconn. and B_Guide/B_Reconn. regimes relevant to Earth's magnetotail.

title: Scaling WaterLily.jl with MPI and an improved geometric multigrid solver
summary: We present recent performance-oriented developments in WaterLily.jl, a scale-resolving incompressible flow solver written in pure Julia that runs seamlessly on CPUs and GPUs of any vendor. Supported by the newly added MPI-based parallelism, strong-scalability tests display a near-ideal linear trend, and weak-scaling efficiency is kept above 85\% before node memory-concurrency contention dominates parallel performance. Inter-node weak scalability is sustained above 96\% with grid size up to 1 billion cells. We further benchmark improvements to the geometric multigrid Poisson solver enabled by an adaptive under-relaxed red-black Gauss--Seidel smoother together with anisotropic coarsening operators.

AI.tldr: WaterLily.jl, a Julia-based incompressible flow solver, now features MPI parallelism achieving near-ideal strong scaling and >96% inter-node weak scaling efficiency up to 1 billion cells, plus an improved multigrid Poisson solver with adaptive smoothing and anisotropic coarsening.
AI.motivation: To enable large-scale, high-performance scale-resolving incompressible flow simulations on heterogeneous CPU/GPU architectures by extending WaterLily.jl with MPI-based parallelism and improving the geometric multigrid Poisson solver.
AI.method: Added MPI-based parallelism to WaterLily.jl; improved the geometric multigrid Poisson solver using an adaptive under-relaxed red-black Gauss-Seidel smoother and anisotropic coarsening operators.
AI.result: Near-ideal linear strong scaling; weak-scaling efficiency above 85% before memory contention, sustained >96% inter-node weak scaling with grids up to 1 billion cells.
AI.conclusion: The developments make WaterLily.jl a highly scalable and efficient solver for large-scale incompressible flow simulations across CPU/GPU platforms.

title: Linear Stability and Jacobi Kernels of Three-Dimensional Sessile Drops
summary: We consider the linear stability of three-dimensional sessile drops with a free contact line. The equilibrium surface is an axisymmetric solution of the Young--Laplace equation with gravity, fixed volume, and prescribed contact angle. We derive the constrained second variation of the gravity--capillary energy and formulate the associated Jacobi problem. Although variational stability gives nonnegativity, the second variation is necessarily degenerate because horizontal translations preserve the energy. Our main result identifies this degeneracy completely. Under the pressure--volume nondegeneracy condition $(dV/dλ\neq 0)$, we prove that the kernel of the constrained Jacobi operator is exactly the two-dimensional space generated by horizontal translations. The proof combines the geometric structure of the Jacobi operator with a Fourier-mode analysis: the axisymmetric mode is ruled out by the pressure--volume condition, the first mode gives translations, and all higher modes are excluded by comparison. This provides the precise linear nondegeneracy underlying stability of droplet dynamics modulo translations.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that under the pressure-volume nondegeneracy condition, the only zero-energy deformations of a sessile drop (with free contact line) are horizontal translations, fully characterizing the kernel of the constrained Jacobi operator.
AI.motivation: To understand the linear stability of three-dimensional sessile drops with a free contact line by identifying the degeneracy in the second variation of the gravity-capillary energy caused by translational invariance.
AI.method: The authors derive the constrained second variation of the energy, formulate the Jacobi problem, and combine geometric structure of the Jacobi operator with Fourier-mode analysis to prove the kernel dimension.
AI.result: Under dV/dλ ≠ 0, the kernel of the constrained Jacobi operator is exactly the 2D space generated by horizontal translations. The axisymmetric mode is excluded by the pressure-volume condition, the first Fourier mode corresponds to translations, and higher modes are ruled out by comparison.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides the precise linear nondegeneracy (modulo translations) underlying stability of droplet dynamics, rigorously establishing the structure of the Jacobi operator's kernel.

title: JAX-FVM: A differentiable, entropy-stable finite volume solver on unstructured meshes for compressible flows
summary: We present JAX-FVM, an open-source, fully differentiable finite volume method (FVM) for the two-dimensional compressible Euler and Navier-Stokes equations on unstructured triangular meshes. The solver is written entirely in JAX, so that every operation : mesh connectivity, flux evaluation, slope limiting, and time integration is just-in-time compiled, vectorised, and end-to-end differentiable through automatic differentiation (AD), and runs transparently on CPU or GPU. On the numerical side, JAX-FVM is built around an entropy-conservative Tadmor/Ismail-Roe two-point flux supplemented with entropy-variable Rusanov or Roe dissipation, second-order MUSCL reconstruction of primitive variables with least-squares gradients and Venkatakrishnan limiting, and a family of explicit (RK2-4) and matrix-free implicit (Newton, SDIRK2) time integrators whose Jacobian actions are obtained by AD. The combination of an unstructured-mesh compressible FVM with end-to-end differentiability fills a gap left by existing differentiable CFD frameworks, which are almost exclusively restricted to structured grids or spectral discretisations. We describe the governing equations, the discretisation, the software architecture, and a set of standard verification cases. The code is openly available at https://github.com/guigzair/jax_fvm.

AI.tldr: JAX-FVM is an open-source, fully differentiable finite volume method for 2D compressible Euler/Navier-Stokes equations on unstructured triangular meshes, written entirely in JAX with end-to-end automatic differentiation, supporting CPU/GPU execution.
AI.motivation: Existing differentiable CFD frameworks are almost exclusively restricted to structured grids or spectral discretisations, leaving a gap for unstructured-mesh compressible FVM with end-to-end differentiability.
AI.method: Built around entropy-conservative Tadmor/Ismail-Roe two-point flux with entropy-variable Rusanov/Roe dissipation, second-order MUSCL reconstruction with least-squares gradients and Venkatakrishnan limiting, explicit (RK2-4) and matrix-free implicit (Newton, SDIRK2) time integrators with Jacobian actions obtained by AD. All operations (mesh connectivity, flux evaluation, slope limiting, time integration) are JIT-compiled, vectorised, and end-to-end differentiable.
AI.result: A differentiable unstructured-mesh compressible FVM solver that runs on CPU/GPU, demonstrated through standard verification cases.
AI.conclusion: JAX-FVM fills the gap in differentiable CFD for unstructured meshes, enabling gradient-based optimization and inverse design for compressible flow problems on complex geometries.

title: Observation of Self-Similarity in the Magnetic Fields Generated by the Ablative Nonlinear Rayleigh-Taylor Instability
summary: Magnetic fields generated by the nonlinear Rayleigh-Taylor growth of laser-seeded three-dimensional broadband perturbations were measured in laser-accelerated planar targets using ultrafast proton radiography. The experimental data show self-similar behavior in the growing cellular magnetic field structures. These observations are consistent with a bubble competition and merger model that predicts the time evolution of the number and size of the bubbles, linking the cellular magnetic field structures with the Rayleigh-Taylor bubble and spike growth.

AI.tldr: This paper measures magnetic fields from laser-driven Rayleigh-Taylor instability growth using proton radiography, observing self-similar cellular magnetic structures that match a bubble merger model.
AI.motivation: To experimentally measure and understand magnetic fields generated by nonlinear Rayleigh-Taylor instability in laser-accelerated targets.
AI.method: Ultrafast proton radiography was used to measure magnetic fields in laser-accelerated planar targets with seeded 3D broadband perturbations.
AI.result: Self-similar behavior was observed in the growing cellular magnetic field structures, with data matching a bubble competition and merger model.
AI.conclusion: The cellular magnetic field structures are linked to Rayleigh-Taylor bubble and spike growth, and the bubble merger model successfully predicts their time evolution.

title: Moiré Phonon Condensation in Magic-Angle Twisted Bilayer Graphene
summary: Twisted bilayer graphene reconstructs from weak breathing corrugation to large common bending near the magic angle, but the origin of this collective crossover has remained unclear. Here we show that the crossover is a soft-mode condensation of layer-symmetric $A_1$ moiré flexural phonons: these modes soften on the breathing branch, lose stiffness near the magic angle, and freeze into the bending morphology. We call this mechanism Moiré Phonon Condensation (MPC). At $θ=1.08^\circ$, it is extremely surprising that displacements of all 11164 atoms in the moiré supercell, with a maximum atomic position shift of 2.30 Angstrom, is captured by only two $A_1$ phonon modes at more than $99.5\%$ spectral weight. A first-harmonic continuum theory identifies a dimensionless control parameter of the phenomenon, showing that as the twist approaches the magic angle, the growing moiré length scale amplifies a smooth stress-bending competition until the flexural stiffness changes sign. Mode-resolved tight-binding calculations further show that the condensed phonon coordinates are electronically active. This work identifies MPC as a twist-controlled structural order parameter for moiré reconstruction.

AI.tldr: Twisted bilayer graphene undergoes a collective crossover from breathing corrugation to common bending near the magic angle, driven by soft-mode condensation of A₁ moiré flexural phonons (Moiré Phonon Condensation). This transition is captured by only two phonon modes at 99.5% accuracy and is electronically active.
AI.motivation: To explain the origin of the structural crossover in twisted bilayer graphene from weak breathing corrugation to large common bending near the magic angle, which has remained unclear despite experimental observation.
AI.method: The authors identify soft-mode condensation of A₁ moiré flexural phonons, develop a first-harmonic continuum theory with a dimensionless control parameter, and perform mode-resolved tight-binding calculations to probe electronic activity.
