AI.result: At θ=1.08°, displacements of all 11,164 atoms in the moiré supercell (max shift 2.30 Å) are captured by only two A₁ phonon modes at >99.5% spectral weight. The flexural stiffness changes sign as twist approaches the magic angle due to amplified stress-bending competition.
AI.conclusion: Moiré Phonon Condensation (MPC) serves as a twist-controlled structural order parameter for moiré reconstruction, with the condensed phonon coordinates being electronically active.

title: On a zero-mass $(p,q)$-Laplacian equation involving subcritical and supercritical growth in $\mathbb R^N$
summary: This paper is concerned with the zero-mass $(p,q)$-Laplacian equation $$ -Δ_p u-Δ_q u = |u|^{r-2}u+λ|u|^{s-2}u \quad \text{in } \mathbb{R}^N, $$ where $1<q<p<N$. The exponents $r$ and $s$ may belong to either the subcritical or the supercritical range with respect to the critical Sobolev exponents $p^*$ and $q^*$. We establish existence and nonexistence results and show that the sign of the parameter $λ$ determines the solvability regimes of the equation. The existence proofs rely on variational methods, truncation arguments, and regularity theory, while the nonexistence results are derived from a suitable Pohozaev-type identity for the $(p,q)$-Laplacian operator.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the zero-mass (p,q)-Laplacian equation in ℝ^N, establishing existence and nonexistence results depending on the sign of parameter λ, using variational methods, truncation arguments, regularity theory, and a Pohozaev-type identity.
AI.motivation: To understand the solvability regimes of the zero-mass (p,q)-Laplacian equation, particularly how the sign of the parameter λ determines existence/nonexistence, and to handle both subcritical and supercritical exponent ranges.
AI.method: Variational methods, truncation arguments, and regularity theory for existence proofs; a Pohozaev-type identity for nonexistence results.
AI.result: Existence and nonexistence results are established; the sign of λ determines whether solutions exist.
AI.conclusion: The parameter λ's sign is the key factor controlling solvability regimes for the zero-mass (p,q)-Laplacian equation across subcritical and supercritical exponent ranges.

title: Compact Rational Krylov for Parametrized Systems with Application to BEM Frequency Sweeping
summary: In parametrized linear systems $\mathsf{P}(μ)\mathsf{x}=\mathsf{b}$ the system matrix $\mathsf{P}$ depends nonlinearly on a parameter $μ$ and solutions are sought for many values of this parameter. We show that the compact rational Krylov (CORK) framework, originally introduced to solve nonlinear eigenvalue problems, can be used to efficiently produce approximate solutions to such a system for many values of the parameter at once. In this approach the parametrized system is first linearized, resulting in a large shifted linear system $(\boldsymbol{\mathsf{A}}-μ\boldsymbol{\mathsf{B}})\boldsymbol{\mathsf{y}}=\boldsymbol{\mathsf{d}}$. We formulate a left- and right-preconditioned rational Krylov GMRES method for shifted linear systems and show how the CORK framework can be used to speed up these methods in the setting of parametrized linear systems. Additionally, we show how to incorporate a right-hand side $\mathsf{b}(μ)$ that also depends on the parameter, how to choose the shifts to steer convergence and how to allow for inexact solves at these shifts throughout the iterations. As an application we consider the 'frequency sweeping' of Helmholtz scattering problems through the Boundary Element Method (BEM), enabled via an efficient representation of the dense but data-sparse wavenumber-dependent system matrix.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the CORK framework, originally for nonlinear eigenvalue problems, to efficiently solve parametrized linear systems P(μ)x=b for many parameter values. It uses linearization, rational Krylov GMRES with preconditioning, and handles parameter-dependent right-hand sides, shift selection, and inexact solves. Application: frequency sweeping of Helmholtz BEM scattering problems.
AI.motivation: Parametrized linear systems arise frequently (e.g., frequency sweeping in Helmholtz scattering), where solutions are needed for many parameter values. Efficiently computing these solutions simultaneously rather than solving each system independently is the key motivation.
AI.method: The parametrized system P(μ)x=b is linearized into a shifted linear system (A - μB)y = d. A left- and right-preconditioned rational Krylov GMRES method is formulated for shifted systems. The CORK framework accelerates these methods, and extensions cover parameter-dependent right-hand sides b(μ), strategic shift selection, and inexact solves at shifts.
AI.result: The CORK framework effectively produces approximate solutions for many parameter values simultaneously, with demonstrated applicability to Helmholtz scattering problems via BEM with data-sparse wavenumber-dependent matrices.
AI.conclusion: The CORK framework is a viable and efficient approach for parametrized linear systems, enabling simultaneous solution across many parameter points with practical extensions for real-world applications like frequency sweeping in acoustics.

title: An Innovative Computational Fluid Dynamics Discrete Dipole Approximation (CFD-DDA) Platform for Predicting Airborne Virus-in-Saliva Disinfection by Ultraviolet Irradiation
summary: All published models of ultraviolet (UV) inactivation of airborne viruses in saliva droplets have neglected UV light scattering. To the best of our knowledge, this work presents the first Computational Fluid Dynamics-Discrete Dipole Approximation (CFD-DDA) platform for investigating the physical mechanisms governing UV disinfection of virus-laden airborne saliva droplets. The DDA solver predicts UV light scattering by both spherical and irregularly shaped saliva droplets, while the CFD solver predicts droplet evaporation and transport in airflow. By coupling the DDA and CFD solvers, we demonstrate that infected saliva droplets, whether spherical or irregularly shaped due to evaporation, experience highly non-uniform UV light scattering that significantly affects virus inactivation and cannot be neglected. This phenomenon has not previously been investigated within a fully three-dimensional framework. The coupled Euler-Lagrange CFD-DDA model further quantifies the effects of (i) the initial droplet size distribution and concentration, (ii) airflow rate, and (iii) droplet interactions with the surrounding airflow and bounding walls on the total number of surviving coronavirus copies $N_s$, assuming a virion diameter of 100 nm, an air temperature of 21 $^{\circ}$C, and a relative humidity of 65%. Based on the DDA results, a new virus inactivation model, referred to as the Dbouk-Yurkin law, is proposed. This model extends the classical Chick-Watson law by explicitly accounting for UV light scattering in both spherical and non-spherical airborne saliva droplets. The proposed three-dimensional CFD-DDA platform provides a powerful framework for improving the understanding of UV-based airborne virus disinfection and for optimizing the design and performance of UV air purification systems.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first CFD-DDA platform to model UV inactivation of airborne viruses in saliva droplets, accounting for UV light scattering—previously neglected. It shows that UV scattering in spherical and irregular droplets significantly affects virus inactivation, and proposes a new Dbouk-Yurkin law extending the classical Chick-Watson model.
AI.motivation: Existing UV inactivation models for airborne viruses in saliva droplets have ignored UV light scattering, leading to incomplete understanding of disinfection mechanisms. This work aims to fill that gap by developing a comprehensive computational framework.
AI.method: A coupled CFD-DDA platform is developed: DDA solver predicts UV light scattering by spherical and irregular saliva droplets; CFD solver predicts droplet evaporation and transport. The coupled Euler-Lagrange model quantifies effects of droplet size, airflow, and wall interactions on virus survival.
AI.result: UV light scattering by saliva droplets (both spherical and irregular) is highly non-uniform and significantly affects virus inactivation. The proposed Dbouk-Yurkin law extends the Chick-Watson law to account for scattering effects. Quantified surviving coronavirus copies under specified conditions (virion diameter 100 nm, 21°C, 65% RH).
AI.conclusion: The CFD-DDA platform provides a powerful framework for understanding UV-based airborne virus disinfection and optimizing UV air purification system design.

title: On the Gross-Pitaevskii model with a moving impurity: Cauchy problem and superfluidity criterion
summary: We study the one-dimensional Gross-Pitaevskii equation with a traveling delta potential and non-zero conditions at infinity. This model describes the effect of a moving impurity in a quantum fluid. Firstly, we show that the associated Cauchy problem is globally well-posed in the energy space. This requires the definition of a conserved energy, which involves the notion of renormalized momentum. Secondly, we study the existence and stability of stationary states in a co-moving reference frame. It is known that there exists an impurity-dependent critical velocity above which no stationary state exists. For velocities below the critical one, two different stationary states appear. We show the orbital stability of the one with higher minimal density.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a moving impurity in a quantum fluid modeled by the Gross-Pitaevskii equation with a traveling delta potential. It proves global well-posedness in the energy space using renormalized momentum, and shows orbital stability of the stationary state with higher minimal density below a critical velocity.
AI.motivation: To rigorously analyze the effect of a moving impurity in a quantum fluid, specifically establishing well-posedness and stability of stationary states in a co-moving reference frame.
AI.method: Uses functional analysis and energy conservation with renormalized momentum to prove global well-posedness in the energy space. Studies stationary states in a co-moving frame, identifying a critical velocity threshold and analyzing stability via orbital stability theory.
AI.result: Global well-posedness in the energy space is established. A critical velocity is identified: above it no stationary states exist; below it two stationary states appear, with the one having higher minimal density being orbitally stable.
AI.conclusion: The moving impurity model is well-posed, and the stationary state with higher minimal density is stable below the critical velocity, providing insight into quantum fluid dynamics under perturbation.

title: An Approximate Bounded Cochain Projection
summary: This paper presents a construction of a projector from an infinite-dimensional Hilbert complex of differential $k$-forms onto a finite-dimensional piecewise polynomial sub-complex. We demonstrate that, on contractable domains, the proposed projector attains the three properties of the Bounded Cochain Projector, namely that the projector is idempotent, uniformly bounded in the Sobolev norm, and it commutes with the exterior derivative. On non-contractible domains, the projector remains idempotent and uniformly bounded, while the commuting property is satisfied up to arbitrary accuracy.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs a projector from an infinite-dimensional Hilbert complex of differential k-forms onto a finite-dimensional piecewise polynomial sub-complex, achieving Bounded Cochain Projector properties (idempotent, uniformly bounded, commutes with exterior derivative) on contractible domains, and maintaining idempotent/uniformly bounded behavior with approximate commuting on non-contractible domains.
AI.motivation: To develop a projector that maps infinite-dimensional differential forms onto finite-dimensional polynomial subspaces while preserving key cohomological structures, enabling practical finite element computations.
AI.method: Construction of a projector from infinite-dimensional Hilbert complex to finite-dimensional piecewise polynomial sub-complex, analyzed on contractible and non-contractible domains.
AI.result: On contractible domains, the projector achieves all three Bounded Cochain Projector properties. On non-contractible domains, it maintains idempotence and boundedness, while commuting property holds only approximately.
AI.conclusion: The proposed projector provides a practical tool for finite element exterior calculus, with exact structure preservation on contractible domains and approximate preservation on more general domains.

title: Dimension-dependent continuum limits in tissue mechanics
summary: Continuum descriptions of epithelial tissue mechanics can replace expensive individual-based simulations with tractable macroscopic models, yet the link between cell-scale forces and tissue-scale transport remains poorly understood. We show that dimensionality controls this link: long-time mechanical relaxation rates reveal generalized porous-media-type nonlinear transport phenomena, $D(ρ)\proptoρ^γ$. Exponents in nonlinear diffusivities are fixed by microscopic mechanics and dimensionality, providing a novel physical mechanism for emergent macroscopic transport phenomena.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that dimensionality controls the link between cell-scale forces and tissue-scale transport, revealing that long-time mechanical relaxation rates exhibit generalized porous-media-type nonlinear transport with exponents fixed by microscopic mechanics and dimensionality.
AI.motivation: To replace expensive individual-based simulations of epithelial tissue with tractable macroscopic continuum models, the link between cell-scale forces and tissue-scale transport needs to be understood.
AI.method: The authors analyze how dimensionality controls the relationship between microscopic mechanics and macroscopic transport, deriving nonlinear diffusivities of the form D(ρ) ∝ ρ^γ, where exponents are determined by both microscopic mechanics and dimensionality.
AI.result: Long-time mechanical relaxation rates reveal generalized porous-media-type nonlinear transport phenomena with exponents in nonlinear diffusivities fixed by microscopic mechanics and dimensionality.
AI.conclusion: Dimensionality provides a novel physical mechanism for emergent macroscopic transport phenomena in epithelial tissues.

title: Boundedness and blow-up for a quasilinear Keller-Segel system with flux limitation and indirect signal production
summary:
The quasilinear Keller-Segel system with flux limitation and indirect signal production u_t=

We conclude that For N=1 and any σ\in\mathbb{R}, if m\geq0, the classical solution exists globally, and it is moreover bounded if m>0. AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the quasilinear Keller-Segel system with flux limitation and indirect signal production, establishing conditions for global existence vs. finite-time blow-up of classical solutions depending on spatial dimension N, parameter m (from degenerate diffusion), and flux limitation exponent σ.
AI.motivation: To understand how flux limitation (parameter σ) and indirect signal production affect the competition between diffusion and chemotactic aggregation, determining whether solutions exist globally or blow up in finite time.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis of the PDE system under Neumann boundary conditions, deriving critical thresholds for parameters (N, m, σ) that separate global existence/boundedness regimes from finite-time blow-up regimes.
AI.result: (i) For N=1: global existence if m≥0, boundedness if m>0; blow-up possible if m<0. (ii) For N≥2, m>1-1/N: if σ ≤ (mN+2-2N)/(2N-2) → global solution; if σ < that threshold → uniformly bounded. (iii) For N≥2, m<2-2/N: if σ > max{N/(2-2N), (mN+2-2N)/(2N-2)} and Ω is a ball → finite-time blow-up possible.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides sharp critical conditions on σ (flux limitation exponent) relative to m (diffusion exponent) and dimension N, delineating when chemotactic collapse is prevented by flux limitation and when blow-up still occurs.

title: A black-box, multilevel algebraic preconditioning framework for conforming finite elements
summary: Recently we introduced the least-squares algebraic-multigrid domain-decomposition (LS-AMG-DD) method as a multilevel, algebraic preconditioner for sparse symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices that admit a Gram representation \(A=G^{\top}G\) \cite{southworth2026lsamgdd}. The factor \(G\) induces a local symmetric positive semidefinite (SPSD) splitting of \(A\) used to define local spectral problems from which an interpolation $P$ is built, and a coarse-level Gram operator induced under Galerkin coarsening, \(A_c=G_c^\top G_c\), for \(G_c:=GP\). This paper clarifies when this Gram structure arises, showing that, on a prescribed degree-of-freedom cover \({\cal C}\), a \({\cal C}\)-local Gram representation of $A$ exists if and only if \(A\) admits a \({\cal C}\)-local SPSD splitting. We then connect this viewpoint to conforming finite-element discretizations, where bilinear forms are naturally assembled from elementwise SPSD energies and therefore admit element-local Gram representations after choosing local factors (e.g., via algebraic factorizations of element blocks). Taken together, these observations provide an essentially black-box route for applying LS-AMG-DD to conforming finite-element problems. Numerical tests illustrate the robustness of the method on several problems for which classical AMG methods require more than $10^5$ iterations to converge, including high-order discretizations of grad--div in \(\hdiv\), anisotropic hyperdiffusion in $H^2$, and linear elasticity in vector \(H^1\). Moreover, in some comparisons with existing AMG methods, LS-AMG-DD produces errors that are 2--5 orders of magnitude smaller, even when all methods are stopped at the same relative residual tolerance.

AI.tldr: This paper clarifies when the Gram structure needed for LS-AMG-DD exists in SPD matrices, linking it to local SPSD splittings and conforming finite-element discretizations, and demonstrates robustness on challenging problems where classical AMG fails.
AI.motivation: To provide theoretical clarity on when the LS-AMG-DD method's required Gram representation exists and to enable its black-box application to conforming finite-element problems.
AI.method: The authors establish that a C-local Gram representation of A exists iff A admits a C-local SPSD splitting, and connect this to conforming finite-element discretizations where bilinear forms are assembled from elementwise SPSD energies.
AI.result: LS-AMG-DD is robust on problems where classical AMG requires >10^5 iterations, including high-order grad-div in H(div), anisotropic hyperdiffusion in H^2, and linear elasticity in H^1. It produces 2-5 orders of magnitude smaller errors at same tolerance.
AI.conclusion: The Gram structure viewpoint provides a black-box route for applying LS-AMG-DD to conforming finite-element problems with excellent robustness and accuracy.

title: Enhanced sampling and cryo-EM data resolve magnesium binding to RNA
comment: Main text: 25 pages (5 figures); Supplementary Information: 24 pages (14 figures, 3 tables)
summary: Magnesium ions are essential for RNA structure but difficult to model due to slow binding kinetics and experimental limitations. We present an enhanced-sampling strategy that accelerates Mg$^{2+}$ inner-shell binding by orders of magnitude, enabling quantitative exploration of ion-binding motifs in a large ribozyme. The method combines a barrier-flattening bias with Hamiltonian replica exchange to efficiently sample multiple equivalent binding sites, and builds on an approach that achieved top performance in the CASP16 blind assessment of RNA solvation structure. Using cryo-electron microscopy maps for validation, we introduce a local analysis framework that infers the population of individual binding motifs from their agreement with experimental density, enabling site-by-site validation. We find that insufficient sampling of inner-shell binding leads to significantly poorer agreement with experiment, whereas force fields predicting different inner/outer binding equilibria remain largely indistinguishable at the current experimental resolution. These results highlight the dominant role of sampling in modelling divalent ion binding and provide a general strategy for integrating simulations with experimental data in complex biomolecular systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an enhanced-sampling method to accelerate Mg²⁺ inner-shell binding simulations, validated with cryo-EM data, showing sampling quality dominates over force-field differences in modeling RNA ion binding.
AI.motivation: Mg²⁺ ions are critical for RNA structure but are difficult to model computationally due to slow binding kinetics and experimental limitations, motivating the need for better sampling strategies.
AI.method: Combines barrier-flattening bias with Hamiltonian replica exchange to accelerate Mg²⁺ inner-shell binding; validated using cryo-EM maps with a local analysis framework for site-by-site population inference.
AI.result: Insufficient inner-shell sampling leads to poor experimental agreement, while different force fields (predicting different inner/outer binding equilibria) remain indistinguishable at current resolution.
AI.conclusion: Sampling quality is the dominant factor in modeling divalent ion binding; the strategy provides a general approach for integrating simulations with experimental data.

title: Nonlocal minimal surfaces are generically unique, and smooth in one extra dimension
summary: We study the fractional Plateau problem for $s$-minimal sets with prescribed exterior datum. For fixed exterior data, minimizers need not be unique, and singularities may occur beyond the critical dimension. We first prove a generic uniqueness theorem: along any strictly increasing family of exterior data, nonuniqueness occurs for at most countably many parameters. We then show that one can make arbitrarily small perturbations for which the interior regularity theory improves by one dimension.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the fractional Plateau problem for s-minimal sets with prescribed exterior data, proving generic uniqueness and improved interior regularity through small perturbations.
AI.motivation: To address non-uniqueness and singularities that occur in the fractional Plateau problem for s-minimal sets beyond the critical dimension.
AI.method: Proves a generic uniqueness theorem using strictly increasing families of exterior data, and demonstrates that arbitrarily small perturbations can improve interior regularity by one dimension.
AI.result: Nonuniqueness occurs for at most countably many parameters along any strictly increasing family of exterior data. Small perturbations can improve interior regularity by one dimension.
AI.conclusion: The results suggest that despite inherent non-uniqueness and singularity issues in s-minimal sets, generic uniqueness holds and regularity can be enhanced through controlled perturbations.

title: Explicit stabilized implementation of singly diagonally implicit Runge-Kutta methods
summary: Implicit methods are a natural approach for the integration of stiff differential equations, to avoid time-step restrictions faced by standard explicit integrators. Explicit stabilised integrators are an alternative to implicit methods, which can be particularly efficient in high-dimensional applications with diffusive terms. Towards the best of both worlds, we introduce a new explicit stabilised implementation of a class of diagonally implicit Runge-Kutta methods. This allows us to implement high-order singly-diagonally-implicit Runge-Kutta methods for advection-diffusion-reaction PDEs with a provable computational cost analogous to that of standard explicit stabilised methods. The main ingredient is to recast the implicit Runge-Kutta update as the steady state of a modified auxiliary system, which is then computed using a partitioned Runge-Kutta-Chebyshev method inspired by optimisation techniques.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new explicit stabilized implementation of diagonally implicit Runge-Kutta methods for stiff PDEs, combining the benefits of implicit and explicit stabilized methods with computational cost similar to explicit stabilized methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome time-step restrictions of explicit integrators for stiff differential equations while avoiding the complexity of fully implicit methods, achieving the best of both worlds.
AI.method: Recasts the implicit Runge-Kutta update as the steady state of a modified auxiliary system, then computes it using a partitioned Runge-Kutta-Chebyshev method inspired by optimization techniques.
AI.result: Achieves high-order singly-diagonally-implicit Runge-Kutta methods for advection-diffusion-reaction PDEs with computational cost analogous to standard explicit stabilized methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed explicit stabilized implementation enables efficient integration of stiff PDEs without typical stability restrictions, offering a provably cost-effective alternative to both pure implicit and pure explicit stabilized approaches.

title: From 2D to 3D: Recovering Turbulent Density Dispersions from Noisy Data
comment: 12 pages, 13 figures, accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: Turbulence plays a central role in shaping the structure and dynamics of the interstellar medium (ISM), governing the star formation rate (SFR) and the initial mass function (IMF). A key consequence of turbulence is the generation of density fluctuations, which regulate the amount of dense gas available for star formation. Accurate measurements of the three-dimensional (3D) turbulent density dispersion are therefore essential for understanding molecular-cloud structure and star formation. However, observations typically provide only two-dimensional (2D) column densities and are often affected by measurement/detector noise. The Brunt method estimates the 3D density dispersion from 2D column-density maps, but it does not account for finite signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Here, we extend the method to recover the 3D turbulent density dispersion from noise-contaminated observations. Using numerical simulations spanning a range of density perturbation amplitudes and noise types, we identify a characteristic noise wavenumber, k_noise, corresponding to the intersection of the signal and noise spectra. Restricting the Brunt reconstruction to wavenumbers below k_noise yields a denoised density-dispersion estimate that closely reproduces the noise-free result. We provide a practical prescription to determine k_noise directly from the measurement SNR and image resolution. Alternatively, if the noise spectrum is known, it can be subtracted directly from the observed spectrum, eliminating the need to estimate k_noise. The proposed correction recovers the noise-free density dispersion with errors of <~5% for SNR>=3 and <~15% for SNR>=1, enabling substantially more reliable estimates of turbulent density fluctuations from noisy column-density data.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the Brunt method to estimate 3D turbulent density dispersion from 2D column-density maps contaminated by noise. By identifying a characteristic noise wavenumber (k_noise) and restricting reconstruction to wavenumbers below it, the method recovers noise-free density dispersion with errors <5% for SNR>=3 and <15% for SNR>=1.
AI.motivation: Accurate measurements of 3D turbulent density dispersion are essential for understanding molecular-cloud structure and star formation, but observations only provide 2D column densities contaminated by noise. The existing Brunt method does not account for finite signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), limiting its applicability.
AI.method: Using numerical simulations with varying density perturbation amplitudes and noise types, the authors identify a characteristic noise wavenumber (k_noise) at the intersection of signal and noise spectra. They propose restricting the Brunt reconstruction to wavenumbers below k_noise, and provide a practical prescription to determine k_noise from measurement SNR and image resolution. An alternative noise-subtraction approach is also offered when the noise spectrum is known.
AI.result: The proposed correction recovers noise-free density dispersion with errors <5% for SNR>=3 and <15% for SNR>=1, enabling substantially more reliable estimates of turbulent density fluctuations from noisy column-density data.
AI.conclusion: The extended Brunt method successfully corrects for noise contamination in turbulent density dispersion estimates, making it applicable to real observational data with finite SNR, and thus improving our understanding of ISM turbulence and star formation.

title: Unconditional Uniqueness for the Energy-critical and Energy-supercritical Quadratic NLS
summary: We study the quadratic nonlinear Schrödinger equation in energy-critical and energy-supercritical regimes and establish unconditional uniqueness at critical regularity on both $\mathbb{T}^{d}$ and $\mathbb{R}^{d}$. We introduce a new infinite quadratic hierarchy that featuring a linear structure for tensor product forms, instead of marginal densities. Consequently, this newly constructed hierarchy differs from the quantum Gross-Pitaevskii hierarchy, and its structure is in fact closer to that of the classical Boltzmann hierarchy. We prove this quadratic hierarchy admits combinatorial structures that are compatible with the bilinear $U$-$V$ estimates we prove for the quadratic nonlinearity. These tools enable us to establish unconditional uniqueness at the critical regularity via a quadratic hierarchy approach, and thus to provide an affirmative answer to Bourgain's uniqueness concern [3,p.152] for the quadratic nonlinearity in the weak-form bilinear case.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes unconditional uniqueness for the quadratic nonlinear Schrödinger equation at critical regularity using a new quadratic hierarchy approach, addressing Bourgain's uniqueness concern.
AI.motivation: To address Bourgain's uniqueness concern for quadratic nonlinearities at critical regularity, and to develop new tools for understanding energy-critical and energy-supercritical regimes.
AI.method: Introduces a new infinite quadratic hierarchy with a linear structure for tensor product forms (differing from the Gross-Pitaevskii hierarchy), proves combinatorial structures compatible with bilinear U-V estimates, and uses a quadratic hierarchy approach to establish unconditional uniqueness.
AI.result: Establishes unconditional uniqueness at critical regularity for the quadratic nonlinear Schrödinger equation on both T^d and R^d, providing an affirmative answer to Bourgain's uniqueness concern for the quadratic nonlinearity in the weak-form bilinear case.
AI.conclusion: The quadratic hierarchy approach successfully resolves the uniqueness problem for quadratic nonlinearities at critical regularity, with the hierarchy structure being closer to the classical Boltzmann hierarchy than the quantum Gross-Pitaevskii hierarchy.

title: Structure-Guided Gauss-Network Method: Linear Advection-Reaction Equation
summary: The least-squares neural network (LSNN) method introduced in [5] for linear advection-reaction equations is capable of accurately approximating discontinuous solutions without a priori knowledge of the interface location. However, the resulting discretization is a non-convex optimization problem that is computationally intensive and complex. In this paper, we propose a structure-guided Gauss-Newton (SgGN) method that alternates between the linear (output) and the nonlinear (hidden layer) parameters. At each outer iteration, the linear parameters are computed by a linear solver, and the nonlinear parameters are updated by a modified Gauss-Newton (GN) method that explicitly removes the singularities of the GN matrix. Numerical experiments for all test problems presented in [5] show that the SgGN method is superior to the Adam optimizer [13], the commonly used first-order optimization algorithm, not only in computational cost but, more importantly, in accuracy

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a structure-guided Gauss-Newton (SgGN) method for least-squares neural networks (LSNN) that efficiently handles non-convex optimization by alternating between linear and nonlinear parameter updates, outperforming Adam in both cost and accuracy for linear advection-reaction equations.
AI.motivation: The LSNN method from prior work can approximate discontinuous solutions well but involves a computationally intensive non-convex optimization problem. The authors aim to improve efficiency and accuracy.
AI.method: A structure-guided Gauss-Newton (SgGN) method that alternates between linear parameters (solved via linear solver) and nonlinear parameters (updated by a modified Gauss-Newton method that removes singularities of the GN matrix).
AI.result: SgGN outperforms the Adam optimizer in both computational cost and accuracy across all test problems from the prior work.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SgGN method provides a more efficient and accurate optimization approach for LSNN-based solutions of linear advection-reaction equations with discontinuous solutions.

title: Asymmetric Focal Loss Improves Graph Neural Network Prediction of Drug-Drug Interactions
summary: Background: Graph neural networks improve computational prediction of polypharmacy side effects, but standard binary cross-entropy training allocates equal capacity to well-classified and difficult examples, potentially missing clinically significant interactions. We evaluated whether an asymmetric focal objective could improve multi-relational drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction by emphasizing difficult positive interactions. Methods: ClinicalFocal loss was integrated into a relation-aware graph convolutional network using molecular fingerprints, physicochemical descriptors, and learned embeddings. The model was evaluated on TWOSIDES using five-fold cross-validation with identical experimental conditions (architecture, features, data partitions, hyperparameters, and random seeds) for ClinicalFocal loss and binary cross-entropy baseline. Results: ClinicalFocal loss increased accuracy from 0.699 to 0.892 (+19.3 percentage points) and F1 score from 0.700 to 0.894 (+19.4 percentage points). AUROC increased from 0.766 to 0.914, and AUCPR increased from 0.714 to 0.860. The false-negative rate decreased from 29.8% to 9.1%, while specificity increased from 69.6% to 87.5%. Overall classification error decreased from 30.1% to 10.8%, corresponding to a 64.1% relative reduction. Improvements were consistent across all five folds. Conclusions: Asymmetric focal optimization improved classification and ranking performance while achieving 90.9% recall for observed interaction triples, without modifying the underlying architecture. Loss-function design is a direct, tunable lever for improving graph-based DDI prediction.

AI.tldr: An asymmetric focal loss (ClinicalFocal) integrated into a relation-aware GCN significantly improves polypharmacy side effect prediction, boosting accuracy from 0.699 to 0.892 and reducing false-negative rate from 29.8% to 9.1% compared to binary cross-entropy.
AI.motivation: Standard binary cross-entropy training allocates equal capacity to well-classified and difficult examples, potentially missing clinically significant drug-drug interactions. The authors sought a loss function that emphasizes difficult positive interactions.
AI.method: ClinicalFocal loss was integrated into a relation-aware graph convolutional network using molecular fingerprints, physicochemical descriptors, and learned embeddings. The model was evaluated on TWOSIDES with five-fold cross-validation under identical conditions (architecture, features, partitions, hyperparameters, seeds) against binary cross-entropy baseline.
AI.result: Accuracy: 0.699→0.892 (+19.3pp); F1: 0.700→0.894 (+19.4pp); AUROC: 0.766→0.914; AUCPR: 0.714→0.860; False-negative rate: 29.8%→9.1%; Specificity: 69.6%→87.5%; Overall error: 30.1%→10.8% (64.1% relative reduction). Consistent across all five folds.
AI.conclusion: Asymmetric focal optimization improves both classification and ranking performance, achieving 90.9% recall for observed interaction triples without modifying the underlying architecture. Loss-function design is a direct, tunable lever for improving graph-based DDI prediction.

title: An existence result for fractional problems with mixed boundary conditions
summary:
In this paper we investigate the existence and qualitative properties of weak solutions for a class of nonlinear fractional equations driven by the spectral fractional Laplacian under mixed Dirichlet--Neumann boundary conditions. More precisely, we consider subcritical problems of the form \begin{equation*} \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
  (-Δ)^s u=λu + μf(x,u) & \text{ in } Ω\smallskip, \\ \displaystyle uχ_{\SD} +\frac{\partial u}{\partialν}χ_{\SN}=0 & \text{ on } \partialΩ, \end{array} \right. \end{equation*} where \(s\in(1/2,1)\), \(Ω\subset\mathbb R^N\) is a bounded smooth domain, and the boundary \(\partialΩ\) is endowed with a mixed Dirichlet--Neumann configuration satisfying suitable geometric and analytic assumptions. Working in the natural spectral fractional framework associated with the mixed boundary operator, we establish the existence of nontrivial weak solutions by means of suitable variational methods. The analysis is based on a topological and variational principle combined with a localization argument involving certain compactly supported cutoff functions. More precisely, the proof relies on a delicate local comparison argument involving localized plateau-type test functions and explicit asymptotic estimates near the origin. Such an approach is sufficiently flexible and may potentially be adapted to several different settings. As applications, we establish simplified existence criteria in the autonomous case through the use of the Chebyshev radius of the domain and present concrete examples illustrating the applicability of the abstract theory.

AI.tldr: This paper proves existence of weak solutions for nonlinear fractional equations with spectral fractional Laplacian under mixed Dirichlet-Neumann boundary conditions using variational methods and a local comparison argument with plateau-type test functions.
AI.motivation: To study existence and qualitative properties of weak solutions for nonlinear fractional equations with mixed boundary conditions, which are less explored than pure Dirichlet or Neumann cases in the fractional setting.
AI.method: Working in the spectral fractional framework associated with mixed boundary operators, using variational methods combined with a topological and variational principle, and a localization argument involving compactly supported cutoff functions and plateau-type test functions with asymptotic estimates near the origin.
AI.result: Existence of nontrivial weak solutions is established under subcritical nonlinearities. Simplified existence criteria for the autonomous case via Chebyshev radius are provided, with concrete examples demonstrating applicability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach is flexible and can potentially be adapted to other settings, offering a robust framework for mixed boundary value problems in fractional equations.

title: Multiple Double Arithmetic on NVIDIA Tensor Cores
comment: accepted for inclusion in the proceedings of ICMS 2026, the International Conference on Mathematical Software
summary:
A multiple double is an unevaluated sum of doubles. An NVIDIA tensor core is a specialized high performance compute core for matrix multiplication. The Ampere A100, released in 2020, introduced tensor cores capable of 64-bit floating-point arithmetic. Every multiple double arithmetical operation requires renormalization, which involves branching, for which tensor cores are unsuited.
  To solve this problem caused by renormalization, we apply a solution similar to the Ozaki scheme [Ozaki et al, Numerical Algorithms, 2012]. Our software is available under the GPU GPL license on github.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a method to implement multiple double arithmetic on NVIDIA tensor cores by avoiding branching through an Ozaki-like scheme, with open-source GPU GPL licensed software available on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Tensor cores in the A100 GPU support 64-bit floating-point but are unsuited for multiple double arithmetic due to the branching required in renormalization, motivating a branch-free solution.
AI.method: The authors apply an Ozaki-like scheme to avoid branching during renormalization, enabling multiple double arithmetic to run efficiently on tensor cores.
AI.result: Software implementation is available under GPU GPL license on GitHub, demonstrating practical feasibility.
AI.conclusion: The Ozaki-like scheme successfully enables multiple double arithmetic on tensor cores, overcoming the branching limitation.

title: Higher-Order Geometric Updates for Levenberg-Marquardt Method via Riemann Normal Coordinates
summary: Nonlinear least-squares optimization is central to regression, physics-informed neural networks, and other machine-learning tasks. Such problems have a natural geometric interpretation, model predictions form a manifold in data space, while the chosen parameterization can introduce parameter-effects curvature that becomes a dominant source of nonlinearity. This exposes a limitation of the Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) method, its tangent-space step is applied as a straight update in parameter coordinates. Geodesic acceleration gives a second-order correction, but its removal of parameter-effect curvature is exact only in the infinitesimal-step limit. We propose a Riemann-normal-coordinate Levenberg-Marquardt method (RNC-LM) to improve this consistency for finite optimization steps. By reformulating the geodesic equation, RNC-LM extends geodesic acceleration to arbitrary-order corrections and constructs finite-step updates with progressively higher reparameterization consistency. A line search along the resulting RNC curve controls the traveled distance while keeping the cost close to standard LM. The method eliminates the tangential component of residual acceleration order by order in a moving tangent frame, making the actual objective reduction more consistent with the linear model prediction of LM. On classical nonlinear least-squares benchmarks, RNC-LM improves convergence and robustness in curved valleys and rank-deficient problems. On a reaction-diffusion PINN failure-mode benchmark, it reduces the relative L2 error to the order of 1e-3 and recovers a physically meaningful solution. On a large-scale machine-learning potential-energy-surface fitting task, it achieves a 34-fold speedup over standard LM.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Riemann-normal-coordinate Levenberg-Marquardt (RNC-LM), which extends geodesic acceleration to arbitrary-order corrections to improve consistency between tangent-space steps and actual objective reduction in nonlinear least-squares optimization, achieving significant improvements on benchmarks and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard Levenberg-Marquardt applies tangent-space steps as straight updates in parameter coordinates, which fails to account for parameter-effects curvature. Geodesic acceleration provides only a second-order correction exact only in the infinitesimal limit, leaving inconsistency for finite optimization steps.
AI.method: RNC-LM reformulates the geodesic equation to extend geodesic acceleration to arbitrary-order corrections, constructs finite-step updates with progressively higher reparameterization consistency, and uses a line search along the RNC curve to control distance while keeping cost close to standard LM.
AI.result: On classical benchmarks, RNC-LM improves convergence and robustness in curved valleys and rank-deficient problems. On a reaction-diffusion PINN benchmark, it reduces relative L2 error to ~1e-3. On a large-scale ML potential-energy-surface fitting task, it achieves a 34-fold speedup over standard LM.
AI.conclusion: RNC-LM effectively eliminates the tangential component of residual acceleration order by order in a moving tangent frame, making actual objective reduction more consistent with the linear model prediction of LM, leading to superior convergence and robustness.

title: $L^p$-Asymptotic Profiles for the Heat Equation with a Hardy Potential
summary: For radial initial data, we construct explicit higher-order asymptotic profiles in $L^p(\mathbb{R}^N)$ to the heat equation with inverse-square potential. These profiles are obtained from the small-argument expansion (up to arbitrary order $n$) of the modified Bessel function appearing in the radial heat kernel. We prove that, after subtracting the profile of order \(n\), the remainder decays faster by an additional power of \(t\). We also describe the non-radial case through spherical harmonics: each angular mode evolves according to a radial Hardy heat equation, leading to finite and infinite angular expansion versions of the asymptotic profile under suitable summability assumptions.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs explicit higher-order asymptotic profiles for the heat equation with inverse-square potential, showing that subtracting these profiles yields faster decay rates, and extends results to non-radial cases via spherical harmonics.
AI.motivation: To understand the refined long-time behavior of solutions to the heat equation with inverse-square potential, going beyond leading-order asymptotics by constructing explicit higher-order correction terms.
AI.method: Uses small-argument expansion of modified Bessel functions in the radial heat kernel to build asymptotic profiles up to arbitrary order n. For non-radial data, applies spherical harmonics decomposition to reduce each angular mode to a radial Hardy heat equation.
AI.result: After subtracting the n-th order profile, the remainder decays faster by an additional power of t. Non-radial cases yield finite and infinite angular expansion versions of the asymptotic profile under summability conditions.
AI.conclusion: Provides a complete asymptotic expansion framework for the heat equation with inverse-square potential in both radial and non-radial settings, with rigorous decay rate improvements.

title: Hybrid Least Squares/Gradient Descent Methods for MIONets
summary: In this paper, we propose an efficient hybrid least squares/gradient descent (LSGD) method for MIONets to accelerate training. This method generalizes the LSGD method for DeepONets. Since MIONet is the sum of the entrywise product of multiple branch networks and a trunk network, it can be viewed as a multilinear function with respect to the last layer parameters of each branch network. These sets of parameters can be optimized using the alternating least squares method, where we solve the LS system for a single branch network in turn. To handle the large-sized system matrix, we introduce Kronecker and Khatri-Rao products and tensor permutation matrices to factor the large matrix into small ones. Our method is compatible with a general type of $L^2$ loss with regularization terms for the last layer parameters of each branch, where linear operators can be applied to the MIONet output in each loss term.

AI.tldr: Proposes LSGD, an efficient hybrid least squares/gradient descent method for training MIONets, which generalizes LSGD for DeepONets by using alternating least squares on branch network parameters.
AI.motivation: To accelerate training of MIONets by exploiting their multilinear structure in the last layer parameters of branch networks.
AI.method: Uses alternating least squares to optimize last layer parameters of each branch network by solving LS systems, with Kronecker/Khatri-Rao products and tensor permutation matrices to handle large system matrices.
AI.result: The method is compatible with general L2 loss with regularization terms and can handle linear operators applied to MIONet outputs.
AI.conclusion: The LSGD method effectively accelerates MIONet training by leveraging its multilinear architecture for efficient parameter optimization.

title: Are Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials Truly Practical? A Benchmark of 23 Mainstream Models
summary: Most MLIP benchmarks reward static accuracy while ignoring inference efficiency and hardware scalability -- driving model bloat with unclear real-world value. We benchmark 23 mainstream open-source MLIPs on a low-cost NVIDIA DGX Spark (128 GB native memory, capped at 80 GB to mimic ordinary lab hardware), using a fixed 192-atom system under a unified ASE-based pipeline. We evaluate three dimensions: predictive accuracy, MD simulation throughput, and atomic scalability. Our results expose a sharp accuracy-efficiency trade-off: large SOTA models deliver only 3-5 meV/atom more accuracy than lightweight ones, but lose orders of magnitude in throughput -- in the worst case, becoming only marginally faster than DFT itself. Lightweight MLIPs, by contrast, sit on the Pareto frontier and run on modest hardware. The lesson is that single-dimensional benchmarks mislead the field, and that future MLIP development should value efficiency and scalability alongside accuracy.

AI.tldr: A benchmark of 23 open-source MLIPs on modest hardware reveals that large models sacrifice orders of magnitude in throughput for marginal accuracy gains (3-5 meV/atom), while lightweight models sit on the Pareto frontier and are far more practical.
AI.motivation: Existing MLIP benchmarks emphasize static accuracy while ignoring inference efficiency and hardware scalability, leading to model bloat with questionable real-world utility.
AI.method: 23 mainstream open-source MLIPs were benchmarked on a low-cost NVIDIA DGX Spark (capped at 80 GB memory) using a fixed 192-atom system under a unified ASE-based pipeline, evaluating predictive accuracy, MD simulation throughput, and atomic scalability.
AI.result: Large SOTA models achieve only 3-5 meV/atom better accuracy than lightweight models but lose orders of magnitude in throughput, sometimes becoming barely faster than DFT. Lightweight MLIPs lie on the Pareto frontier and run on modest hardware.
AI.conclusion: Single-dimensional benchmarks mislead the field; future MLIP development should value efficiency and scalability alongside accuracy.

title: Symmetry-breaking and local stability of a two-phase eigenvalue problem in optimal insulation
summary: We consider the first eigenvalue, $λ_β(Ω,A)$, of a two-phase eigenvalue problem for the Laplacian with Robin boundary conditions, where the two phases are characterised by different ellipticity constants. We characterise the conditions under which a ball $B_R$ is a local minimum under a volume constraint for the minimisation problem $A\mapstoλ_β(B_r,A)$, in terms of the principal Neumann eigenvalue of the fixed inner ball $B_r$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how the first eigenvalue of a two-phase Laplacian with Robin boundary conditions behaves under volume constraints, identifying when a ball serves as a local minimum based on Neumann eigenvalues of an inner ball.
AI.motivation: To characterize conditions under which a ball is a local minimizer for a two-phase eigenvalue problem with Robin boundary conditions under volume constraints.
AI.method: The authors analyze the first eigenvalue λ_β(Ω,A) of a two-phase Laplacian with Robin boundary conditions, where phases have different ellipticity constants. They study the minimization problem A↦λ_β(B_r,A) under volume constraints and relate it to the principal Neumann eigenvalue of the fixed inner ball B_r.
AI.result: Conditions are characterized for which a ball B_R is a local minimum under volume constraint, expressed in terms of the principal Neumann eigenvalue of the inner ball B_r.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a precise relationship between two-phase Robin eigenvalue problems and Neumann eigenvalues, providing local minimality conditions for balls in such problems.

title: Topological Signatures of Diffusive Release in Porous Media
summary: We used persistent homology to quantify the multiscale topological and geometric organization of porous media, including solid connectivity and the formation of loop-like and cavity-like structures across spatial scales. Through statistical analysis, we show that these topological and geometric features are closely associated with diffusion-driven release behavior in porous media. In particular, even within each target-porosity level, samples with richer topological features tend to exhibit long-tailed release, indicating that release behavior depends not only on the amount of pore space but also on the multiscale organization of the solid phase. We further show that persistent homology-based features can classify release-curve regimes using a simple classification model. Notably, feature extraction is substantially faster than finite element diffusion simulations. Together, these results suggest that persistent homology provides a lightweight, interpretable, and geometry-based descriptor for screening diffusive release behavior in porous media.

AI.tldr: Persistent homology quantifies multiscale pore geometry in porous media, showing strong links to diffusion-driven release behavior; it enables fast, interpretable classification of release regimes.
AI.motivation: To understand how multiscale topological and geometric features of porous media (beyond just porosity) influence diffusive release behavior, and to find a lightweight alternative to expensive finite element simulations.
AI.method: Apply persistent homology to extract topological features (connectivity, loops, cavities) across scales; use statistical analysis and a simple classification model to relate these features to diffusion-driven release curves.
AI.result: Topological features correlate strongly with long-tailed release; classification models based on persistent homology can distinguish release regimes; feature extraction is much faster than finite element simulations.
AI.conclusion: Persistent homology is a lightweight, interpretable, geometry-based descriptor for screening diffusive release behavior in porous media.

title: Pic2Spec: Generative Modeling Reconstructs Single Cell Raman Fingerprints from Brightfield Images
summary: Single-cell molecular characterization remains a bottleneck in scalable biological analysis because of labeling requirements, limited multiplexing, and reagents that perturb physiology. Raman spectroscopy addresses these limits by providing chemically specific, label-free vibrational fingerprints, but long acquisition times and specialized instruments restrict high-throughput use. Here, we overcome this barrier by showing that spectral fingerprints can be reconstructed from brightfield microscopy using generative modeling. We introduce Pic2Spec, a framework that learns a shared latent biochemical representation linking image morphology to vibrational spectral structure, enabling virtual Raman spectroscopy without hardware. We validate Pic2Spec across mammalian and bacterial cells, generating high-fidelity spectra that reproduce measured Raman fingerprints with 98% cosine similarity and Pearson correlations of ~95%, while preserving biochemical peaks and population distributions. Beyond spectral similarity, Pic2Spec provides molecular-level resolution in bacterial systems: generated spectra discriminate mutation-driven transgenic states and predict GFP expression with accuracy approaching true Raman measurements, outperforming conventional image analysis by 20%. These findings establish Pic2Spec as a first demonstration of chemically informative virtual molecular fingerprinting from brightfield images, complementing slow, hardware-intensive spectroscopy with computational inference. By redefining microscopy as an inference-enabled molecular profiling platform, Pic2Spec democratizes label-free biochemical phenotyping and overcomes the hardware and time constraints that have confined spectroscopy to specialized laboratories. This enables high-throughput molecular analysis for clinical diagnostics, screening, and monitoring at the scale and accessibility of standard microscopy.

AI.tldr: Pic2Spec is a generative AI framework that reconstructs Raman spectral fingerprints from brightfield microscopy images, achieving 98% spectral similarity and enabling label-free molecular profiling without specialized hardware.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of Raman spectroscopy (slow acquisition, specialized instruments, low throughput) by enabling molecular fingerprinting from standard brightfield microscopy images.
AI.method: Pic2Sec learns a shared latent biochemical representation between image morphology and vibrational spectral structure using generative modeling, then reconstructs Raman spectra from brightfield images.
AI.result: Achieves 98% cosine similarity and ~95% Pearson correlation with measured Raman spectra; discriminates transgenic states and predicts GFP expression with 20% better accuracy than conventional image analysis.
AI.conclusion: Pic2Spec is the first demonstration of chemically informative virtual molecular fingerprinting from brightfield images, democratizing label-free biochemical phenotyping for high-throughput molecular analysis.

title: Regularity for eigenvalue-type equations in sets with thick complement
summary: We prove global higher integrability of the gradient for solutions to nonlinear PDEs with homogeneous Dirichlet condition. We work with general open sets (i.e. not necessarily bounded), under a minimal thickness assumption of the complement. The main focus is on improving the global summability of the gradient, by preserving the ``zero trace" Sobolev class. We pay due attention to providing precise a priori estimates. As an application, we get universal global Hölder estimates for solutions of the super-homogeneous Lane-Emden equation for the $N-$Laplacian, in contractible open sets with finite inradius.

AI.tldr: This paper proves improved global integrability (higher summability) of gradients for solutions to nonlinear PDEs with homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions on general open sets (not necessarily bounded) with a minimal thickness condition on the complement. The main novelty is preserving the "zero trace" Sobolev class while improving gradient regularity, with precise a priori estimates. An application yields universal Hölder estimates for the super-homogeneous Lane-Emden equation for the N-Laplacian on contractible open sets with finite inradius.
AI.motivation: To extend global higher integrability results for gradients of PDE solutions from bounded domains to more general open sets (unbounded) under minimal geometric assumptions, while carefully preserving the zero-boundary Sobolev class.
AI.method: The paper uses functional analysis and PDE techniques, imposing a minimal thickness condition on the complement of the domain. It provides precise a priori estimates and preserves the "zero trace" Sobolev space framework.
AI.result: Global higher integrability of the gradient is established for nonlinear PDEs with Dirichlet conditions on general open sets. As an application, universal global Hölder estimates are derived for the super-homogeneous Lane-Emden equation involving the N-Laplacian.
AI.conclusion: The work improves existing regularity theory by relaxing domain boundedness assumptions, requiring only minimal complement thickness and finite inradius (for the application). The precise estimates and preservation of the zero-trace class are key contributions.

title: The Stability of the Backward Problem for Photoacoustic Imaging in Attenuating Media via Carleman Estimates
summary: This paper investigates the backward problem in time for photoacoustic tomography (PAT) in attenuating media. It is well-established that photoacoustic imaging in attenuating media can be accurately modeled by spatial fractional-order damping. This inverse problem is ill-posed in the sense of Hadamard. In this work, we construct a novel class of Carleman estimates independent of spatial variables, and by virtue of these estimates, we establish conditional stability estimates for this problem for the first time. Building upon this, we propose a Tikhonov-type regularization functional and derive its associated adjoint system. Furthermore, leveraging the established conditional stability results, we derive the convergence rate of the proposed regularization approach. Finally, we validate the effectiveness of our theoretical findings through extensive numerical experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper develops Carleman estimates for the backward problem in photoacoustic tomography with fractional damping, establishes conditional stability, proposes a Tikhonov regularization method with convergence rate, and validates results numerically.
AI.motivation: To address the ill-posed backward problem in photoacoustic tomography (PAT) when media exhibit attenuation, which is modeled by spatial fractional-order damping.
AI.method: Constructs spatial-variable-independent Carleman estimates, uses them to prove conditional stability, proposes a Tikhonov regularization functional with its adjoint system, and derives convergence rates from stability results.
AI.result: First-time conditional stability estimates for the backward PAT problem in attenuating media, convergence rates for Tikhonov regularization, and numerical validation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Carleman-based framework effectively regularizes the ill-posed inverse problem with theoretically guaranteed convergence rates and demonstrated numerical performance.

title: Impact of Channel Dynamics on Higher-order Interactions of Oscillators
summary: Modeling higher-order interactions (HOIs) in nonlinear networks with static topologies is often physically restrictive. We demonstrate that standard 3-body Kuramoto couplings are mathematically equivalent to pairwise connections modulated by latent variables of transmission channels. While standard HOI topologies emerge in the adiabatic limit of these variables, relaxing this constraint reveals that latent channel timescales dictate collective macroscopic states. Specifically, transmission inertia drives bistability for symmetric interaction tensors and anti-phase cluster synchronization for antisymmetric ones. Furthermore, dynamically induced clustering in global topologies emerges as a finite-size effect of the dynamics of the local channels. Ultimately, we show that relying exclusively on static topologies restricts interaction modeling. Integrating latent variables captures the transient inertia and fundamental asymmetry of physical networks, bridging the analytical utility of higher-order functions with the reality of the underlying transmission medium.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that higher-order interactions (HOIs) in nonlinear networks can be mathematically reduced to pairwise connections with latent transmission channel variables. The timescales of these channels determine macroscopic states like bistability and anti-phase synchronization, and finite-size effects of local channel dynamics induce clustering in global topologies.
AI.motivation: Modeling higher-order interactions with static topologies is physically restrictive; the authors aim to show that incorporating latent variables (transmission channel dynamics) better captures the transient inertia and asymmetry of physical networks while retaining analytical utility.
AI.method: The authors demonstrate mathematical equivalence between standard 3-body Kuramoto couplings and pairwise connections modulated by latent channel variables. They analyze the adiabatic limit and relax this constraint to study how channel timescales affect collective behavior, including bistability, anti-phase synchronization, and clustering.
AI.result: Latent channel timescales dictate macroscopic states: transmission inertia drives bistability for symmetric tensors and anti-phase cluster synchronization for antisymmetric tensors. Dynamically induced clustering in global topologies arises as a finite-size effect of local channel dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Relying exclusively on static topologies restricts interaction modeling. Integrating latent variables bridges higher-order analytical frameworks with the physical reality of transmission media.

title: POO-LPSP: Parallel Osprey Optimized Least Penalty-Squared Prioritization Methods for Priority Derivation in the Analytic Hierarchy Process
summary: Pairwise comparison (PC) via pairwise reciprocal matrices (PRMs) is central to the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP). Although the traditional eigenvector method is widely applied to derive priorities, its theoretical robustness in reflecting true priority vectors remains debated. Building upon a previous iteration of this study, this research develops the revised Least Penalty-Squared Prioritization (LPSP) optimization models, including the revised Least Product of Penalty and Direct Squares (LPPDS) and revised Weighted Squares (LPPWS), to minimize the revised Root Mean Penalty-Squared Variance (RMPSV) and the revised Root Mean Penalty-Weighted Square Variance (RMPSWV). However, solving these non-linear formulations is computationally complex for decision-makers. To overcome these limitations, this study proposes the Parallel Osprey Optimized Least Penalty-Squared Prioritization (POO-LPSP) method. By integrating an improved bio-inspired metaheuristic Parallel Osprey Optimization Algorithm (POOA), this framework efficiently solves complex LPSP models to minimize RMPSV and RMPSWV, thereby enhancing prioritization reliability. The practical utility and computational efficiency of the POO-LPSP method are validated through a numerical application focusing on a Generative AI (GAI) vendor selection problem. To extend, POO-LPSP can serve as a robust alternative to Saaty's Eigen system method for AHP applications.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes the Parallel Osprey Optimized Least Penalty-Squared Prioritization (POO-LPSP) method to solve complex non-linear prioritization optimization models in AHP, validated through a GAI vendor selection problem.
AI.motivation: The eigenvector method for pairwise comparison in AHP has debated theoretical robustness. Existing LPSP optimization models are non-linear and computationally complex for decision-makers.
AI.method: Develops revised LPSP models (LPPDS, LPPWS) minimizing RMPSV/RMPSWV, and proposes POO-LPSP integrating the Parallel Osprey Optimization Algorithm (POOA) to efficiently solve these formulations.
AI.result: Validated through a numerical application on a Generative AI vendor selection problem, demonstrating practical utility and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: POO-LPSP serves as a robust alternative to Saaty's Eigen system method for AHP prioritization.

title: Semiclassical Schrödinger operators with purely imaginary potential
summary: We consider Schrödinger operators with purely imaginary potential $P = - h^{2} Δ+ i V ( x )$ on a bounded domain. Assuming that near its critical points the potential $V$ can be approximated by an homogeneous polynomial, we show that in the limit $h \to 0$ the leftmost eigenvalues of $P$ are asymptotically given by the local model associated to the most degenerated critical points of $V$. We give applications of this result to the associated evolution problem including shear flows in fluid mechanics.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes Schrödinger operators with purely imaginary potentials on bounded domains, showing that as h→0, the leftmost eigenvalues are asymptotically determined by the most degenerate critical points of the potential V, with applications to shear flows in fluid mechanics.
AI.motivation: To understand the asymptotic behavior of eigenvalues for non-self-adjoint Schrödinger operators with imaginary potentials, particularly how degenerate critical points govern the spectral properties in the semiclassical limit.
AI.method: The authors assume V can be approximated by a homogeneous polynomial near its critical points, then analyze the local model associated with the most degenerate critical points to derive eigenvalue asymptotics in the limit h→0.
AI.result: The leftmost eigenvalues of P are asymptotically given by the local model associated to the most degenerated critical points of V as h→0.
AI.conclusion: This spectral result has applications to the associated evolution problem, particularly for shear flows in fluid mechanics.

title: Circuit Depth Reduction of One-Ancilla Quantum Differential Equation Solver via Extrapolation
summary: Solving linear differential equations is a fundamental task in scientific computing and an important primitive for quantum computing. A recent one-ancilla quantum differential equation solver provides a hardware-friendly and locality-preserving approach with provable performance guarantees, making it highly suitable for the early fault-tolerant and near-term regimes. Its simple circuit structure comes with a natural trade-off: the maximum single-run circuit depth scales as $O (1/ε)$ in the target accuracy $ε$. In this work, we reduce this depth by combining the solver with classical step-size postprocessing. By running the one-ancilla solver at a logarithmic number of finite time step sizes and using classical post-processing to cancel leading discretization errors, we reduce the maximum single-run circuit depth to $O(\mathrm{polylog}(1/ε))$ without adding quantum ancillae or sacrificing locality. Technically, extending extrapolation ideas beyond Hamiltonian and Lindbladian dynamics requires regularity estimates for observable maps under nonunitary evolution, which we obtain through a holomorphic extension of the adjoint evolution. Numerical experiments on the Hatano-Nelson model (ODE) and the convection-diffusion equation (PDE) demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach.

AI.tldr: This paper reduces the quantum circuit depth of a one-ancilla differential equation solver from O(1/ε) to O(polylog(1/ε)) by combining it with classical step-size postprocessing (Richardson extrapolation), without adding extra qubits or sacrificing locality.
AI.motivation: Solving linear differential equations is fundamental in scientific and quantum computing. A recent one-ancilla quantum solver offers hardware-friendly, locality-preserving advantages suited for early fault-tolerant regimes, but its maximum single-run circuit depth scales unfavorably as O(1/ε) in target accuracy ε, limiting its practical use.
AI.method: The authors combine the one-ancilla quantum solver with classical step-size postprocessing (Richardson extrapolation). They run the solver at a logarithmic number of finite time step sizes and use classical post-processing to cancel leading discretization errors. They extend extrapolation ideas beyond Hamiltonian/Lindbladian dynamics by deriving regularity estimates for observable maps under nonunitary evolution via a holomorphic extension of the adjoint evolution.
AI.result: Maximum single-run circuit depth is reduced from O(1/ε) to O(polylog(1/ε)) without adding quantum ancillae or sacrificing locality. Numerical experiments on the Hatano-Nelson model (ODE) and the convection-diffusion equation (PDE) validate the approach.
AI.conclusion: Classical step-size postprocessing can dramatically reduce the quantum circuit depth requirement of the one-ancilla solver, making it more practical for near-term quantum computing while maintaining its hardware-friendly and locality-preserving properties.

title: Dyadic potential theory and de Rham functions
summary: We study de Rham functional equations driven by two increasing fractional linear transformations. Our main purpose is to relate the singularity theory of the associated solutions to dyadic potential theory on the binary tree. We first prove an existence and uniqueness theorem for increasing, left-continuous solutions in the full range of linear fractional data, and identify the trapping region in parameter space where the solution is continuous. For a large class of parameters we show that the de Rham solution is the normalized cumulative capacitary function of a multiplicative dyadic capacity. This gives a potential-theoretic model for Möbius de Rham systems. We then sharpen Okamura's Hausdorff-dimensional estimates for the singular measure associated with the solution by replacing Hausdorff dimension with dyadic Riesz capacities. In particular, we prove capacitary upper and lower bounds for the size of Borel sets carrying full or zero mass, and obtain a capacitary sharpening of Okamura's singularity criterion.

AI.tldr: This paper studies de Rham functional equations driven by fractional linear transformations, linking solution singularity to dyadic potential theory on binary trees, proving existence/uniqueness results, and sharpening Okamura's Hausdorff estimates using dyadic Riesz capacities.
AI.motivation: To relate the singularity theory of de Rham functional equation solutions to dyadic potential theory on binary trees, and to sharpen existing Hausdorff-dimensional estimates via capacitary methods.
AI.method: The authors prove existence/uniqueness of increasing left-continuous solutions, identify trapping regions in parameter space, show that the de Rham solution equals the normalized cumulative capacitary function of a multiplicative dyadic capacity, and use dyadic Riesz capacities to refine Okamura's estimates.
AI.result: A potential-theoretic model for Möbius de Rham systems is established, with capacitary upper/lower bounds for Borel sets carrying full or zero mass, and a capacitary sharpening of Okamura's singularity criterion.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a deeper connection between de Rham functional equations and dyadic potential theory, yielding refined singularity analysis through capacitary rather than purely Hausdorff-dimensional measures.

title: Inverse Scattering from Conformal Infinity for Totally Geodesic Defects in Hyperbolic Space
summary:
We study inverse scattering from conformal infinity for impenetrable topological defects whose interaction surfaces are totally geodesic in hyperbolic space \(\mathbb H^n\), \(n \geq 2\). For a fixed spectral parameter \(λ_0 > 0\), the prescribed inputs are boundary labels \(ξ\in \partial_\infty \mathbb H^n\). Each label selects an incoming Helgason mode in the hyperbolic interior. Given a defect \(\mathcal P \Subset \mathbb H^n\), this mode generally fails to satisfy the homogeneous trace condition on the interaction surface and hence generates an outgoing correction. The leading coefficient of this correction at conformal infinity defines the measured far-field pattern. The central question is whether \(\mathcal P\) can be recovered from the far-field patterns corresponding to one or finitely many prescribed boundary labels. This gives a formally determined inverse problem at one fixed spectral parameter.
  Our first main result establishes the unique determination of totally geodesic defects, an admissible class that includes both bulk components and hypersurface-supported ones. For Dirichlet-type defects, a single boundary label suffices. For Neumann-type defects, \(n+1\) boundary labels are sufficient and in general necessary. These labels are required to satisfy the natural affine-independence condition at conformal infinity.
  Our second main result provides quantitative stability estimates within the same framework. The hyperbolic Hausdorff distance between two defects is controlled by the discrepancy of their far-field patterns at conformal infinity. The proof combines continuation from conformal infinity with quantitative geodesic reflection across totally geodesic hypersurfaces. Taken together, these results yield a qualitative and quantitative far-field inverse scattering theory at conformal infinity, based on formally determined data.

AI.tldr: This paper studies inverse scattering from impenetrable topological defects in hyperbolic space, proving that totally geodesic defects can be uniquely and stably recovered from far-field patterns at conformal infinity using finitely many boundary labels at a fixed spectral parameter.
AI.motivation: To determine whether impenetrable topological defects (with totally geodesic interaction surfaces) in hyperbolic space can be uniquely and stably reconstructed from far-field measurements at conformal infinity, using only one or finitely many prescribed boundary labels at a fixed spectral parameter—a formally determined inverse problem.
AI.method: The analysis combines continuation from conformal infinity with quantitative geodesic reflection across totally geodesic hypersurfaces. For Dirichlet-type defects, a single boundary label suffices; for Neumann-type, n+1 boundary labels with affine independence are required. Stability estimates control hyperbolic Hausdorff distance via far-field pattern discrepancy.
AI.result: First result: unique determination of totally geodesic defects—one boundary label for Dirichlet, n+1 for Neumann (with affine independence, shown to be necessary). Second result: quantitative stability estimates relating hyperbolic Hausdorff distance to far-field pattern discrepancy.
AI.conclusion: This establishes a complete qualitative and quantitative far-field inverse scattering theory at conformal infinity based on formally determined data, for impenetrable topological defects with totally geodesic interaction surfaces in hyperbolic space.

title: An optimal control approach for neural network architecture adaptation with a posteriori error estimation
summary: This work presents a novel approach for adapting neural network architecture along the depth based on a posteriori error estimation. By formulating neural network training as a continuous-time optimal control problem, we derive rigorous error estimates that quantify how approximation error distributes across network layers. This error decomposition enables a principled depth adaptation strategy: new layers are inserted at locations of maximum estimated error, allowing the network to efficiently capture complex, nonlinear variations in the underlying problem. Our framework introduces a novel network architecture that treats weights and biases as piecewise linear functions varying across layers, with the error estimator bounding the discrepancy between this discrete representation and the true continuous optimal control solution. The approach leverages dual weighted residual methodology from finite element analysis to derive computable upper bounds on the functional error. A key theoretical contribution is the derivation of explicit error bounds that decompose the total approximation error into interval-wise contributions, providing a rigorous basis for targeted architecture refinement. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on scientific datasets, including learning the observable-to-parameter map for the Navier-Stokes equation. Numerical results reveal that our approach consistently outperforms existing architecture adaptation methods in terms of generalization performance.

AI.tldr: A novel depth-adaptive neural network method using a posteriori error estimation, derived from continuous-time optimal control and dual weighted residual methodology, that inserts layers where error is largest and outperforms existing methods on scientific datasets.
