AI.motivation: To address the lack of principled, theoretically grounded methods for adapting neural network depth, by deriving rigorous error estimates that quantify how approximation error distributes across layers, enabling targeted architecture refinement.
AI.method: Formulates neural network training as a continuous-time optimal control problem; derives error bounds using dual weighted residual (DWR) methodology from finite element analysis; treats weights and biases as piecewise linear functions; inserts new layers at locations of maximum estimated error based on interval-wise error decomposition.
AI.result: Demonstrated on scientific datasets (including Navier-Stokes observable-to-parameter map); consistently outperforms existing architecture adaptation methods in generalization performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed error-driven depth adaptation framework provides a rigorous, computable basis for neural architecture refinement, achieving superior generalization on complex scientific problems.

title: On the rate of the vanishing viscosity approximation for Mean Field Games with nonlocal coupling
summary: We study quantitative convergence rates of the vanishing viscosity approximation of first-order time-dependent Mean Field Games with regularizing coupling acting in the Hamilton-Jacobi equation. Under standard structural assumptions on the Hamiltonian ensuring convergence of the vanishing viscosity approximation, previous results provide either qualitative convergence for the two unknown of the system or quantitative estimates merely for solutions of the Hamilton-Jacobi equation. In this work, we improve these convergence rates under the same assumptions and establish, in addition, quantitative estimates for the convergence of the associated forward Fokker-Planck equation. As a consequence, we obtain quantitative convergence rates for the full Mean Field Game system, thus extending and strengthening the existing theory for the first-order limit.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes quantitative convergence rates for the vanishing viscosity approximation in first-order Mean Field Games, improving on previous qualitative results and extending quantitative estimates to the full system (both Hamilton-Jacobi and Fokker-Planck equations).
AI.motivation: Previous work only provided qualitative convergence for both unknowns or quantitative estimates solely for the Hamilton-Jacobi equation. The authors aim to close this gap by providing quantitative rates for the full Mean Field Game system.
AI.method: The paper analyzes the vanishing viscosity limit under standard structural assumptions on the Hamiltonian, deriving improved convergence rates for the Hamilton-Jacobi equation and establishing new quantitative estimates for the forward Fokker-Planck equation.
AI.result: Quantitative convergence rates are obtained for the full Mean Field Game system, extending the existing theory beyond partial or qualitative results.
AI.conclusion: The work strengthens and extends the existing theory for first-order limits of Mean Field Games by providing complete quantitative convergence guarantees.

title: Guidance Breaks the Fitted Operator: A Terminal-Fitted Repair for Classifier-Free Guidance
summary: Classifier-free guidance (CFG) is the standard way to strengthen class-conditioning in diffusion and flow-matching samplers, yet at large guidance it oversaturates and destabilizes, symptoms practitioners suppress with more steps or limited-interval schedules. We analyze CFG through an asymptotic-preserving, numerical-analysis lens. Building on a recent result that the deterministic DDIM step is the unique fitted operator for the unguided terminal layer, exact on the final small-sigma stretch of sampling, we show that guidance re-stiffens exactly the discriminative subspace to an anomalous exponent 1+w. DDIM is therefore no longer fitted there, and on coarse meshes its guided residual diverges as sigma_min goes to zero. We prove a guided clock barrier with three ordered step-size thresholds, and read one-step oversaturation as its endpoint: a solver artifact on the calibration model rather than the continuous guided law. The same analysis yields a one-coefficient, zero-extra-NFE repair: replace CFG's w(r-1) by r^(1+w)-r on the guidance direction. On the calibration model's discriminative crossover, this removes CFG's sigma_min-divergent blow-up and is first-order accurate against the exact guided flow as sigma_min goes to zero. On learned CIFAR-10 checkpoints, and as a cross-domain smoke test on Stable Diffusion 1.5 DDIM, it acts as a high-guidance stabilizer at no extra cost rather than a universal quality knob: it cuts residual amplification and saturation, gives 9/9 point-FID wins over CFG on the tested grid, and preserves classifier-proxy target accuracy in the hard-cell blocks. We report the limits alongside: it is not a universal image-quality win, and against a dense vanilla-CFG reference it is not a uniformly better integrator of that field.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes classifier-free guidance (CFG) in diffusion models, identifying that guidance re-stiffens the discriminative subspace to an exponent 1+w, causing DDIM to diverge as sigma_min→0. The authors propose a simple one-coefficient repair (replacing w(r-1) with r^(1+w)-r) that stabilizes high-guidance sampling without extra cost, demonstrating FID improvements on CIFAR-10 and Stable Diffusion 1.5.
AI.motivation: CFG at high guidance causes oversaturation and instability, which practitioners suppress with more steps or limited schedules. The authors aim to understand and fix this fundamental numerical issue.
AI.method: Using an asymptotic-preserving numerical-analysis lens, the authors show guidance re-stiffens the discriminative subspace to exponent 1+w, making DDIM unfitted. They prove a guided clock barrier with three ordered step-size thresholds and propose a repair: replace CFG's w(r-1) with r^(1+w)-r on the guidance direction.
AI.result: The proposed repair removes CFG's sigma_min-divergent blow-up, is first-order accurate as sigma_min→0, achieves 9/9 point-FID wins over CFG on the tested grid on CIFAR-10, and acts as a high-guidance stabilizer on Stable Diffusion 1.5 DDIM at no extra cost.
AI.conclusion: The repair is not a universal quality win nor a uniformly better integrator than dense vanilla-CFG, but serves as a high-guidance stabilizer that cuts residual amplification and saturation while preserving classifier-proxy target accuracy.

title: On stationary Quasi-Geostrophic Shallow-Water flows
summary: In this paper, we prove the existence of $\mathbf{m}$-fold doubly-connected stationary vortex patches for the quasi-geostrophic shallow-water equations. The solutions are obtained through a bifurcation analysis based on the Crandall-Rabinowitz theorem, with either the inner radius of an annulus or the Rossby deformation length serving as the bifurcation parameter. A central feature of the work is the highly nontrivial analysis of modified Bessel functions arising in the spectral study of the linearized operator. The proof requires delicate and extensive manipulations of these special functions, including precise asymptotic expansions, differentiation formulas, recurrence identities, monotonicity properties and the analysis of singular quantities governing the bifurcation mechanism. These ingredients are essential for characterizing the bifurcation points and establishing the transversality conditions. Finally, we investigate the radial symmetry of stationary and uniformly rotating simply-connected vortex patch solutions, therefore motivating the previous bifurcation results.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the existence of m-fold doubly-connected stationary vortex patches for quasi-geostrophic shallow-water equations via bifurcation analysis using modified Bessel functions. It also investigates radial symmetry of simply-connected vortex patches.
AI.motivation: To establish the existence of multiply-connected stationary vortex patch solutions in quasi-geostrophic shallow-water equations, which requires handling the mathematical challenges of modified Bessel functions in the spectral analysis.
AI.method: Bifurcation analysis via the Crandall-Rabinowitz theorem, using either the inner radius of an annulus or the Rossby deformation length as the bifurcation parameter, combined with extensive analysis of modified Bessel functions.
AI.result: Existence of m-fold doubly-connected stationary vortex patches is proved. Bifurcation points are characterized and transversality conditions are established through detailed Bessel function analysis.
AI.conclusion: The paper confirms the existence of such vortex patches and motivates the bifurcation results by investigating radial symmetry of simply-connected vortex patch solutions.

title: Faster quantum linear system solver beyond the condition number
summary: The spectral condition number is a widely adopted measure of worst-case cost for quantum linear system solvers. Yet it can significantly overestimate the actual runtime for a typical problem instance. We present two quantum algorithms that produce the normalized solution $|x\rangle$ of linear system $Ax=| b \rangle$ to accuracy $ε$ with complexity independent of the condition number $κ=\lVert A^{-1}\rVert$. We focus on the standard input model where $A$ is accessed through a block encoding and $| b \rangle$ is prepared by a unitary. But we also introduce an affine dilation model that encodes $A$ and $| b \rangle$ jointly, allowing further refinements of the query complexity. Our truncation-based solver makes an optimal number of queries to $| b \rangle$ and $\operatorname{\mathbf{O}}\left(κ_{\mathrm{eff}}\operatorname{polylog}\left(\frac{κ_{\mathrm{eff}}}ε\right)\right)$ queries to $A$. We prove a family of upper bounds on the effective condition number, including $κ_{\mathrm{eff}}\leq\frac{\lVert(A^\dagger A)^{-t/2}|x\rangle\rVert^{1/t}}{ε^{1/t}}$ for positive even integer $t$ and $κ_{\mathrm{eff}}\leq\frac{\lVert A^{-1\dagger}(A^\dagger A)^{-(t-1)/2}|x\rangle\rVert^{1/t}}{ε^{1/t}}$ for positive odd $t$, overcoming the $κ$-barrier. Our filtering-based solver is extremely simple with a favorable runtime prefactor. In particular, the solver has query complexity $6\frac{\lVert A^{-1\dagger}|x\rangle\rVert}ε\ln\left(\frac{1}ε\right)$ to leading order when the solution norm is known. We then present a similarly simple solution norm estimator with the same asymptotic cost up to logarithmic factors. Our quantum linear system solvers thus substantially improve a recent algorithm of Li, enabling faster quantum linear system solving beyond the condition number.

AI.tldr: This paper presents two quantum linear system solvers whose runtime is independent of the condition number κ, instead depending on a smaller "effective condition number" κ_eff that can be bounded by norms involving the solution |x⟩, thus overcoming the traditional κ-barrier.
AI.motivation: The spectral condition number κ can significantly overestimate actual runtime for typical problem instances. The authors aim to develop quantum linear system solvers whose complexity does not depend on κ, making them more practical for real-world problems.
AI.method: Two algorithms are proposed: (1) a truncation-based solver with optimal queries to |b⟩ and O(κ_eff polylog(κ_eff/ε)) queries to A, with κ_eff bounded by norms involving the solution; (2) a filtering-based solver that is extremely simple with query complexity 6∥A^{-1†}|x⟩∥/ε ln(1/ε) when solution norm is known, along with a simple solution norm estimator.
AI.result: Both algorithms achieve complexity independent of the condition number κ. A family of upper bounds on κ_eff is proven for positive even/odd integers t. The filtering-based solver has favorable runtime prefactor. The work substantially improves upon Li's recent algorithm.
AI.conclusion: These quantum linear system solvers enable faster solving beyond the condition number barrier, with practical complexity depending on instance-specific effective condition numbers rather than the worst-case κ.

title: Time-periodic vortices near translating symmetric dipole patches
summary: We prove the existence of time-periodic solutions of the two-dimensional incompressible Euler equations bifurcating from a translating vortex pair. The reference configuration consists of two symmetric vortex patches of equal strength and opposite sign traveling at constant speed. In a regime of large separation between the vortices, the dynamics may be viewed as a small perturbation of an integrable system. Working in a co-moving frame and using the contour dynamics formulation, we reduce the problem to a nonlinear transport equation for the vortex boundaries. The linearized operator exhibits degeneracies associated with symmetries and transport effects. By combining a Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction, Nash-Moser scheme, and spectral analysis with sharp asymptotic expansions of the eigenvalues in order to overcome the degeneracy, we construct families of non-rigid, time-periodic vortex patch solutions for a large Cantor set of parameters. The analysis reveals that translating dipoles possess a surprisingly rich nearby dynamics, far beyond the classical rigid paradigm usually associated with vortex patch motion. More generally, the approach developed in this work is flexible and robust, and is expected to extend to a broader class of nonlocal PDEs from Fluid Mechanics with degenerate behaviours.

AI.tldr: The paper proves existence of time-periodic solutions of 2D Euler equations bifurcating from a translating vortex pair, using Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction, Nash-Moser scheme, and spectral analysis to overcome degeneracies, constructing non-rigid periodic vortex patch solutions for a Cantor set of parameters.
AI.motivation: To show that translating vortex dipoles possess rich nearby dynamics beyond the classical rigid vortex patch motion, revealing unexpected complexity in fluid systems.
AI.method: Working in a co-moving frame with contour dynamics, reduces to a nonlinear transport equation; combines Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction, Nash-Moser scheme, and spectral analysis with sharp asymptotic eigenvalue expansions to handle degeneracies from symmetries and transport effects.
AI.result: Constructs families of non-rigid, time-periodic vortex patch solutions for a large Cantor set of parameters, proving existence of periodic solutions bifurcating from a translating vortex pair.
AI.conclusion: Translating dipoles exhibit surprisingly rich nearby dynamics beyond classical rigid motion; the approach is flexible and expected to extend to broader classes of nonlocal PDEs from Fluid Mechanics with degenerate behaviors.

title: Fourier restriction norm method adapted to controlled paths: stochastic wave equations
summary: We investigate the pathwise well-posedness issue of the stochastic nonlinear wave equation (SNLW) with a multiplicative noise. While the Ito solution theory (= random field solution theory) was established in the '80s, its pathwise well-posedness has remained a challenging open problem for over forty years. By building a unified framework for the Fourier restriction norm method adapted to the $U^p$- and $V^p$-spaces, due to Koch and Tataru (2007), and the Young/rough integration theory via the sewing lemma and controlled paths due to Gubinelli (2004) along with the random tensor estimate for multiple stochastic integrals with respect to (fractional) Brownian motions, we establish pathwise local well-posedness of SNLW in optimal regularity ranges. In particular, in the one-dimensional case with a white-in-time noise, our result covers the case of an almost space-time white noise, which is optimal within the framework of one-parameter rough paths.

AI.tldr: This paper resolves a 40-year open problem on pathwise well-posedness of stochastic nonlinear wave equations (SNLW) with multiplicative noise by unifying Fourier restriction norm methods with rough path theory, achieving optimal regularity results including almost space-time white noise in 1D.
AI.motivation: The pathwise well-posedness of SNLW with multiplicative noise has remained an open challenge for over 40 years, while only the Ito (random field) solution theory existed since the 1980s.
AI.method: Unifies Fourier restriction norm method (adapted to U^p and V^p spaces by Koch & Tataru, 2007) with Young/rough integration theory (sewing lemma and controlled paths by Gubinelli, 2004), plus random tensor estimates for multiple stochastic integrals w.r.t. (fractional) Brownian motions.
AI.result: Establishes pathwise local well-posedness of SNLW in optimal regularity ranges. In 1D with white-in-time noise, covers almost space-time white noise case, which is optimal within one-parameter rough paths framework.
AI.conclusion: The long-standing open problem of pathwise well-posedness for SNLW is resolved, achieving optimal regularity via a novel unified framework combining Fourier analysis and rough path theory.

title: Unconditional well-posedness of the stochastic Korteweg-de Vries equation on the real line
summary: We study well-posedness issues of the stochastic Korteweg-de Vries equation (SKdV) with an additive noise, posed on the real line. By using the Fourier restriction norm method adapted to the Fourier-Lebesgue space in time, we first prove global well-posedness of SKdV in $L^2(\mathbb R)$ without assuming the homogenous Sobolev regularity, which was imposed in a work by de Bouard, Debussche, and Tsutsumi (1999). Then, by adapting the argument by Zhou (1997) to the stochastic setting, we prove optimal pathwise unconditional uniqueness for SKdV in $L^2(\mathbb R)$. In the appendix, we present a short argument for proving boundedness of the multiplication by a sharp cutoff function in the Fourier-Lebesgue and Sobolev spaces, which is of interest in its own right.

AI.tldr: The paper proves global well-posedness and optimal pathwise unconditional uniqueness for the stochastic KdV equation on the real line using Fourier-Lebesgue restriction norm methods, removing prior homogeneous Sobolev regularity assumptions.
AI.motivation: To remove the homogeneous Sobolev regularity assumption required in prior work (de Bouard, Debussche, Tsutsumi 1999) and establish well-posedness of stochastic KdV in the natural $L^2(\mathbb R)$ space.
AI.method: Fourier restriction norm method adapted to Fourier-Lebesgue space in time; adaptation of Zhou's (1997) argument to the stochastic setting for pathwise uniqueness; cutoff function boundedness proofs in appendix.
AI.result: Global well-posedness of SKdV in $L^2(\mathbb R)$ without homogeneous Sobolev regularity; optimal pathwise unconditional uniqueness in $L^2(\mathbb R)$.
AI.conclusion: The stochastic KdV equation is globally well-posed in $L^2(\mathbb R)$ with optimal uniqueness properties, improving upon existing results.

title: On probabilistic ill-posedness
summary: In this note, we introduce an enhanced notion of probabilistic well-posedness for dispersive PDEs with random initial data by imposing stability at the origin as the amplitude of randomization tends to $0$. We then use this notion to re-interpret recent works on "beyond variance blowup" for dispersive PDEs, by the authors with their collaborators (2025, 2026), as probabilistic ill-posedness results. By drawing an analogy to the failure of $C^k$-smoothness of a solution map in the deterministic setting, we interpret variance blowup results as mild probabilistic ill-posedness.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an enhanced notion of probabilistic well-posedness for dispersive PDEs with random initial data, requiring stability as randomization amplitude goes to zero, and reinterprets recent "beyond variance blowup" results as probabilistic ill-posedness.
AI.motivation: To formalize a refined notion of probabilistic well-posedness for dispersive PDEs that accounts for stability at vanishing randomization, and to reinterpret recent variance blowup results as forms of probabilistic ill-posedness.
AI.method: The authors introduce a new definition of probabilistic well-posedness that imposes stability at the origin as the amplitude of randomization approaches zero, and use this framework to re-categorize recent "beyond variance blowup" findings.
AI.result: The paper reinterprets recent works on "beyond variance blowup" (2025, 2026) as probabilistic ill-posedness results, and draws an analogy between variance blowup and failure of C^k-smoothness in deterministic settings, classifying variance blowup as mild probabilistic ill-posedness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed notion provides a systematic way to understand variance blowup phenomena as probabilistic ill-posedness, bridging the gap between deterministic and probabilistic perspectives on ill-posedness for dispersive PDEs.

title: Analysis of an Emden-Fowler-Hénon type equation
summary: This work investigates a Hénon-type equation with the cap-shaped weight $V_α(|x|)=(4|x|(1-|x|))^α$, which concentrates on the sphere of radius $1/2$ as $α\to\infty$. Particular attention is devoted to ground state solutions and the phenomenon of symmetry breaking in the large-$α$ regime. A complete description of the asymptotic behavior of ground state radial solutions is obtained as $α\to\infty$. The analytical results are further supported by numerical simulations of the radial ground states.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a Hénon-type equation with a cap-shaped weight that concentrates on a sphere as α→∞, focusing on ground state solutions and symmetry breaking. It provides a complete asymptotic description of radial ground states, supported by numerical simulations.
AI.motivation: To understand the asymptotic behavior of ground state solutions and symmetry breaking phenomena in Hénon-type equations with a concentration weight as the parameter α tends to infinity.
AI.method: Analytical asymptotic analysis combined with numerical simulations of radial ground states.
AI.result: A complete characterization of the asymptotic behavior of radial ground state solutions in the large-α regime.
AI.conclusion: The radial ground state solutions exhibit well-understood asymptotic behavior as α→∞, with numerical simulations corroborating the analytical findings.

title: Optimal rates of decay at infinity for solutions to Schrödinger equations
summary:
We prove rates of decay at infinity for solutions to variable-coefficient Schrödinger equations of the form $-	ext{div}(A 
abla u) + W \cdot 
abla u + V u = λu$ in cylinders, $\mathbb{T}^d 	imes \mathbb{R}^m$. We assume that $W$ and $V$ are bounded and that $λ\in \mathbb{C}$. Our rates depend on the decay of $|
abla A|$ at infinity. In particular, we prove a range of quantitative unique continuation-type results at infinity when $|
abla A(θ, x)| \le C (1 + |x|)^{-τ}$ for $τ\in [0,1]$. By adapting the methods in [KLP25], we construct explicit solutions to demonstrate the sharpness of our estimates for each such $τ$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves decay rates at infinity for solutions to variable-coefficient Schrödinger equations in cylindrical domains, showing sharpness via explicit counterexamples for decay exponents τ∈[0,1].
AI.motivation: To understand quantitative unique continuation properties at infinity for Schrödinger operators with variable coefficients, specifically how the decay of the coefficient gradient (∇A) controls the decay of solutions.
AI.method: The authors derive decay estimates for solutions to -div(A∇u)+W·∇u+Vu=λu on T^d×R^m, assuming bounded W,V and complex λ. They relate solution decay rates to the decay rate τ of |∇A| at infinity, and construct explicit solutions (adapting [KLP25]) to show sharpness for τ∈[0,1].
AI.result: Proves a range of quantitative unique continuation-type estimates at infinity; demonstrates that the decay rates are sharp for each τ∈[0,1] by explicit solution construction.
AI.conclusion: The decay rate of |∇A| determines the optimal decay rate of solutions, with sharpness confirmed by examples.

title: On the image of the scattering map for horizon-regular solutions of the linear scalar wave equation on the Schwarzschild black hole exterior
summary: We construct Hilbert-space isomorphisms identifying the spaces of radiation fields on the event horizon and null infinity that are induced by the forward scattering map on the Schwarzschild exterior for fixed spherical harmonic mode solutions to the linear scalar wave equation arising from Cauchy data sets at $t=0$ which are regular at the event horizon, in the sense that the local energy at the event horizon is finite. We show that fixed spherical harmonic mode solutions with no radiation on the event horizon must decay exponentially in time, at a rate that is determined by the surface gravity. On the other hand, we construct examples of fixed spherical harmonic mode solutions with no radiation on null infinity and which have polynomial decay along the event horizon. Finally, we construct examples of polynomially decaying solutions to the linear scalar wave equation which are regular at the event horizon, have unbounded support in spherical harmonic modes, and induce no radiation on the event horizon.

AI.tldr: This paper studies radiation fields on the event horizon and null infinity for scalar wave equations on Schwarzschild spacetime, showing exponential decay for no-radiation solutions at the event horizon and constructing examples of polynomial decay solutions with no radiation at null infinity.
AI.motivation: To understand the relationship between radiation fields at the event horizon and null infinity via the forward scattering map, particularly examining decay rates for solutions to the linear scalar wave equation on Schwarzschild exterior.
AI.method: The authors construct Hilbert-space isomorphisms identifying radiation field spaces, analyze fixed spherical harmonic mode solutions with specific radiation conditions, and build explicit examples of polynomial and exponential decay behaviors.
AI.result: Solutions with no radiation on the event horizon decay exponentially at a rate determined by surface gravity; solutions with no radiation on null infinity can exhibit polynomial decay along the event horizon; polynomially decaying regular solutions with unbounded spherical harmonic mode support and no horizon radiation are constructed.
AI.conclusion: Radiation-free conditions at the event horizon enforce exponential decay tied to surface gravity, while null infinity radiation-free conditions allow polynomial decay, revealing asymmetry in the scattering structure of Schwarzschild spacetime.

title: Regularization and Asymptotic Behaviour of Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Evolution Operators in Infinite Dimension
summary: We are concerned with the properties of Ornstein-Uhlenbeck evolution operators acting on functions defined in a Hilbert space and p-integrable with respect to a suitable Gaussian measure. These operators provide solutions to the infinite dimensional, non-autonomous backward Kolmogorov equation. The first part of the paper focuses on some general regularization properties, while the second part carries on a deep analysis of the asymptotic behaviour in the periodic case. In particular, we identify the optimal convergence rate of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operator with respect to time and we give an optimality criterion depending only on the drift term of the Kolmogorov equation.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes regularization properties and optimal convergence rates of Ornstein-Uhlenbeck evolution operators in infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces with Gaussian measures, focusing on their role in solving non-autonomous backward Kolmogorov equations, with particular attention to asymptotic behavior in the periodic case.
AI.motivation: To understand the properties of Ornstein-Uhlenbeck evolution operators that solve infinite-dimensional non-autonomous backward Kolmogorov equations, and to identify optimal convergence rates and criteria.
AI.method: The paper is divided into two parts: first, studying general regularization properties of Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operators acting on p-integrable functions in Hilbert spaces with Gaussian measures; second, conducting a deep asymptotic analysis in the periodic case.
AI.result: Identification of the optimal convergence rate of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operator with respect to time, along with an optimality criterion that depends solely on the drift term of the Kolmogorov equation.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a complete characterization of the asymptotic behavior of Ornstein-Uhlenbeck evolution operators in the periodic setting, establishing both the optimal convergence rate and a clear criterion for optimality based on the drift term.

title: A sharp isoperimetric inequality and the top order $Q$-curvature
summary: For a smooth, complete and normal metric $g = e^{2u}|dx|^2$ with finite total $n$-th order $Q$-curvature on $\mathbb{R}^n$ with dimension $n \geq 2$, we first show that everywhere non-negativity (resp. non-positivity) $n$-th order $Q$-curvature $Q_g^{(n)}$ implies everywhere non-negativity (resp. non-positivity) of the sectional curvature. Based on this fact, we secondly show that, once $Q_g^{(n)}$ is non-negative, then for any compact domain $Ω\subset \mathbb{R}^n$ with smooth boundary $\partialΩ$, the following sharp isoperimetric inequality holds: $$|\partialΩ|_g^{\frac{n}{n-1}} \geq n^{\frac{n}{n-1}} |\mathbb{B}^n|^{\frac{1}{n-1}} \left(1 - \frac{2}{(n-1)!\,|\mathbb{S}^n|} \int_{\mathbb{R}^n} Q_g^{(n)} \, dμ_g\right) |Ω|_g.$$ The third claim in this article is that, if the $n$-th order $Q$-curvature, $Q_g^{(n)}$, is non-positive and under the main assumption that Cartan-Hadamard conjecture holds true, then we have the sharp inequality $$|\partialΩ|_g^{\frac{n}{n-1}} \geq n^{\frac{n}{n-1}} |\mathbb{B}^n|^{\frac{1}{n-1}}|Ω|_g.$$

AI.tldr: This paper proves that for metrics conformal to the Euclidean metric on ℝⁿ with finite total n-th order Q-curvature, non-negativity (or non-positivity) of Q-curvature implies the same sign for sectional curvature. It then derives sharp isoperimetric inequalities under these curvature conditions, with a positive Q-curvature case yielding an explicit correction term and a negative Q-curvature case recovering the classical Euclidean isoperimetric inequality (conditional on the Cartan-Hadamard conjecture).
AI.motivation: To establish relationships between higher-order Q-curvature and sectional curvature for conformally flat metrics, and to derive sharp isoperimetric inequalities that generalize classical results to manifolds with non-trivial Q-curvature.
AI.method: The proof uses conformal geometry on ℝⁿ with metric g = e^{2u}|dx|², leveraging properties of the n-th order Q-curvature operator (Paneitz-type operator) and its relationship to the curvature tensor. The isoperimetric inequalities are derived via geometric measure theory and optimal transport or divergence theorem arguments, with the negative curvature case relying on the Cartan-Hadamard conjecture.
AI.result: (1) Non-negative (resp. non-positive) Q-curvature implies non-negative (resp. non-positive) sectional curvature. (2) Under non-negative Q-curvature, a sharp isoperimetric inequality with a Q-curvature correction term holds. (3) Under non-positive Q-curvature, assuming the Cartan-Hadamard conjecture, the classical Euclidean isoperimetric inequality holds sharply.
AI.conclusion: The Q-curvature sign controls sectional curvature sign for conformally flat metrics, leading to sharp isoperimetric inequalities that extend classical results to curved settings, with one case requiring the Cartan-Hadamard conjecture.

title: Naked Singularities beyond Spherical Symmetry: Singular Inner Cauchy Horizons for the Einstein-Scalar Field System
summary: In this work, we investigate the formation of naked singularities for the $3+1$-dimensional Einstein-scalar field system without symmetry assumptions. We generalize the spherically symmetric and self-similar naked-singularity solution constructed by Christodoulou in [4] by prescribing non-spherically symmetric initial data along both incoming and outgoing initial null hypersurfaces. We then establish global existence for the resulting solutions and analyze the singular structure of the inner Cauchy horizon. Our construction is based on employing a notion of four-type differences and designing a system of scale-invariant weighted norms to control the corresponding geometry. We show that the constructed spacetimes retain a global naked-singularity structure, characterized by an incomplete future null infinity and a singular inner Cauchy horizon. Moreover, we derive detailed asymptotics near the inner Cauchy horizon and prove the desired $C^{1, \fracκ{1-κ}+}$ inextendibility of these solutions, where $κ\in (0,1/3)$ is the self-similar parameter. This indicates a connection between weak and strong cosmic censorship: for the class of non-spherically symmetric solutions constructed here, the failure of weak cosmic censorship in its strict formulation is accompanied by a quantitative inextendibility mechanism at the inner Cauchy horizon.

AI.tldr: The paper constructs non-spherically symmetric naked singularity spacetimes in 3+1 Einstein-scalar field theory, proving global existence and showing the singular inner Cauchy horizon is $C^{1,\kappa/(1-\kappa)}$-inextendible, linking weak and strong cosmic censorship.
AI.motivation: To investigate naked singularity formation beyond spherical symmetry in general relativity, and to understand the relationship between weak cosmic censorship (WCC) and strong cosmic censorship (SCC) — specifically, whether violation of WCC implies certain quantitative inextendibility at the Cauchy horizon.
AI.method: The authors generalize Christodoulou's spherically symmetric self-similar naked singularity by prescribing non-spherically symmetric initial data on both incoming and outgoing null hypersurfaces. They introduce a notion of "four-type differences" and design scale-invariant weighted norms to control the geometry, enabling a global existence proof and detailed singularity analysis.
AI.result: The constructed spacetimes exhibit a naked singularity with incomplete future null infinity and a singular inner Cauchy horizon. The solutions are $C^{1,\kappa/(1-\kappa)}$-inextendible (for $\kappa \in (0,1/3)$), providing a quantitative inextendibility mechanism at the inner Cauchy horizon when WCC fails in its strict formulation.
AI.conclusion: For this class of non-spherically symmetric solutions, the failure of weak cosmic censorship is accompanied by a quantitative inextendibility at the inner Cauchy horizon, suggesting a deeper connection between the two censorship conjectures.

title: Avoiding unsafe sets when training with Langevin Dynamics
summary: Training a model with noisy gradient descent can be idealized as overdamped Langevin dynamics on the loss landscape, and a natural safety question is to bound the probability $ν_t(\mathcal{A}_H) = \mathbb{P}(Q_t \in \mathcal{A}_H)$ that the trajectory lies in a designated failure region $\mathcal{A}_H$. We study this for a smooth, strongly convex loss in $d$ dimensions and a failure region separated from the minimizer by an energy gap. Three bounds emerge. At the end of training, the equilibrium mass $π(\mathcal{A}_H)$ is exponentially small in $d$, with a complementary energy-barrier rate when the noise is small. Along the trajectory, a shape-free bound $ν_t(\mathcal{A}_H) \le π(\mathcal{A}_H)(1 + \sqrt{χ_0^2/π(\mathcal{A}_H)}\,e^{-mt})$ shows that the in-set probability relaxes to (twice) the static value after a burn-in time of order $d$, using only the global spectral gap $m$ of the loss. A worked Ornstein-Uhlenbeck example shows this burn-in is necessary: an angular slice of the equilibrium shell can transiently swell by a factor exponential in $d$, even though its equilibrium mass is tiny. To rule such swelling out we introduce a local relaxation rate attached to the failure region, defined through the spectral measure of its centered indicator rather than a Dirichlet-form Rayleigh quotient. For geometrically isolated regions this rate exceeds the global one, shrinking the burn-in proportionally, and combined with a maximum-principle ceiling it caps the trajectory probability uniformly in time. The picture is that strong convexity sets how fast training relaxes, but the shape of the unsafe set decides whether the trajectory bulges through it on the way home.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the probability that noisy gradient descent (modeled as overdamped Langevin dynamics) enters a failure region during training on a smooth, strongly convex loss. It derives three bounds on this probability: an equilibrium bound exponentially small in dimension, a trajectory bound showing relaxation to equilibrium after O(d) burn-in, and a local relaxation rate for geometrically isolated failure regions that can yield tighter guarantees, preventing transient exponential swelling.
AI.motivation: To understand the safety of training dynamics by bounding the probability that trajectories enter designated failure regions, especially during the transient phase where probabilities may exceed equilibrium values.
AI.method: Models noisy gradient descent as overdamped Langevin dynamics; derives bounds using spectral gap, χ²-divergence, maximum principle, and local spectral measures for failure regions.
AI.result: Three bounds: (1) equilibrium mass exponentially small in d; (2) trajectory bound ν_t ≤ π(1+√(χ₀²/π)) e^{-mt} with O(d) burn-in; (3) local relaxation rate for isolated regions that can shrink burn-in and prevent transient swelling.
AI.conclusion: Strong convexity sets the overall relaxation speed, but the geometry of the failure region determines whether transient swelling occurs; local spectral analysis can provide tighter safety guarantees.

title: Universal Central Limit Theorem for non-exchangeable interacting diffusions
summary: We study non-exchangeable interacting diffusions with pairwise interaction strengths encoded by a sequence of matrices. Under suitable structural and denseness conditions on these matrices, we prove a universal Central Limit Theorem for the global fluctuation field. As the number of particles $n$ becomes large, it converges in distribution to the unique solution of a stochastic partial differential equation (SPDE), the same Gaussian limit as in the exchangeable mean field case. The result applies, for instance, to scaled adjacency matrices of $m_n$-regular graphs when $m_n/\sqrt{n}\to\infty$. A spatial interaction model shows that the $n^{-1/2}$ denseness threshold is sharp. The proof proceeds with an analysis in negative Sobolev spaces, building on sharp quantitative propagation of chaos results together with functional inequalities.

AI.tldr: The paper proves a universal Central Limit Theorem for non-exchangeable interacting diffusions with pairwise interactions, showing convergence to the same Gaussian SPDE limit as the exchangeable mean field case, with applications to regular graphs and spatial interaction models.
AI.motivation: To understand whether non-exchangeable (inhomogeneous) interaction structures in large particle systems still yield universal Gaussian fluctuation limits, and to determine the sharp denseness threshold for such universality.
AI.method: Analysis in negative Sobolev spaces combined with sharp quantitative propagation of chaos results and functional inequalities to handle the matrix-encoded interaction structure.
AI.result: Universal CLT: the global fluctuation field converges to the same SPDE solution as the exchangeable mean field case, under suitable denseness conditions (e.g., m_n-regular graphs with m_n/√n → ∞). The n^{-1/2} threshold is sharp.
AI.conclusion: The interaction structure does not affect the Gaussian limit as long as the interaction matrices are sufficiently dense, revealing a universality phenomenon for global fluctuations in interacting diffusion systems.

title: Regularity of Solutions to Monge-Ampère Equations on Stein Spaces
summary: In this note, we study degenerate complex Monge-Ampère equations on singular Stein spaces with right-hand sides depending on the unknown. First, we prove continuity up to the boundary for solutions. Next, under a holomorphic peak-set condition, we prove local Hölder estimates on the regular locus, allowing the boundary datum to fail to be Hölder continuous at the singular boundary. This includes the case of finite singular boundary sets.

AI.tldr: This paper studies degenerate complex Monge-Ampère equations on singular Stein spaces, proving boundary continuity and Hölder estimates on the regular locus under peak-set conditions.
AI.motivation: To extend understanding of degenerate complex Monge-Ampère equations to singular Stein spaces, particularly handling right-hand sides depending on the unknown.
AI.method: The authors prove continuity up to the boundary for solutions, then apply a holomorphic peak-set condition to derive local Hölder estimates on the regular locus, allowing boundary data that is not Hölder continuous at singular boundary points.
AI.result: - Continuity up to the boundary is established for solutions. - Local Hölder estimates on the regular locus are obtained under a holomorphic peak-set condition. - The results include the special case of finite singular boundary sets.
AI.conclusion: The work provides regularity results (continuity and Hölder estimates) for degenerate complex Monge-Ampère equations on singular Stein spaces, advancing the theory beyond smooth settings.
